confirmed.title: Boss-round transition is a permanent soft-lock: the shop/boss state ping-pongs back to 'shop' one frame after the boss fight starts, forever
confirmed.symptom: After finishing round 4 (or 9, 14, ...), the player reaches the between-rounds shop and clicks 'Continue ->'. The screen appears to ignore the click and simply shows the shop again (or, if buying an upgrade, the purchase happens but they're still dumped back at the shop). No boss fight is ever playable, no error is shown, and the round counter never advances past 4 — the player is permanently stuck and can never reach round 5 or later.
confirmed.rootCause: startBoss() (called from advanceFromShop() at line 757) sets game.state='boss' but never resets game.roundTimer — only startRound() does that (line 809: `game.roundTimer=ROUND_TIME`). Meanwhile update()'s round-over check at line 444 (`if(game.roundTimer<=0){...}`) applies unconditionally whenever update() runs, and update() runs for both 'playing' and 'boss' states per the main loop's gating (`if(state==='playing'||state==='boss') update(dt)`). Since roundTimer was already <=0 (that's what triggered the shop in the first place, at line 451) and is never refreshed by startBoss(), the very first update() call after entering 'boss' state immediately re-triggers the round-over branch. Because game.round was never incremented (advanceFromShop doesn't increment it either — only startRound(r) sets game.round=r, and that's never called on the shop->boss path), isBossRound(game.round+1) is still true, so line 451 sets state back to 'shop' again. This repeats every time the player clicks Continue.
confirmed.evidence: Test suite games/advice-whack/../test_advice-whack.js, section 'CRITICAL: shop -> boss-round transition'. After g.startRound(4); g.game.roundTimer=1; one update(16) frame -> state correctly becomes 'shop' (game.round=4). Dispatching a real mousedown on the canvas at the shop's drawn Continue-button coordinates (480,419) correctly flips state to 'boss' (PASS: stateRightAfterClick === 'boss'). Simulating exactly one more real animation frame (calling update(16) because state is 'boss', matching the real loop's own gating) immediately reverts it: expected state==='boss' but observed state flipped from 'boss' back to 'shop' after just one update(16) frame; game.round is still 4 (never incremented past 4), game.roundTimer is 0. A follow-up loop of 5 repeated Continue-click attempts confirmed the boss state never stabilizes even briefly, and game.round stayed pinned at 4 throughout every attempt.
confirmed.fix:
Minimal correct fix — gate the round-over check to the playing state only. At line 444:

  if(game.state==='playing' && game.roundTimer<=0){

The boss round is meant to end via bossDefeated() (L324), not via the round clock, so the timer must not be able to end it. I verified gate-only on the pristine file: state stays 'boss' with bossHP=9 across the full loop, and bossDefeated()->'roundover'->startRound(round+1) then advances to round 5 correctly.

Recommended companion (cosmetic, not required for the lock): add `game.roundTimer=ROUND_TIME;` in startBoss() (after L202), because drawHUD() renders the timer during 'boss' (L774) and would otherwise show a frozen 0.

EXPLICIT WARNING — the obvious fix alone leaves a second path broken. If you fix ONLY by adding `game.roundTimer=ROUND_TIME;` to startBoss() and leave line 444 ungated, the soft-lock just moves: I verified this. With that patch, 500ms into the boss the state correctly holds ('state=boss round=4 timer=59488 bossHP=9'), but when the boss clock runs out (60s — reachable if the player is slow on a 9-HP boss) the ungated L444 fires again during 'boss', game.round is still 4, isBossRound(5) is still true, and it dumps back to the shop ('state=shop round=4 timer=0 bossHP=9') — re-entering the same loop with the boss reset to full HP. With the L444 gate applied, the same expired-timer scenario correctly holds 'state=boss'. So the state gate is the load-bearing change; the timer reset is not a substitute for it.

confirmed.title: The core action (placing a weight) reliably ends the game before a player can ever recover, while never touching the screen wins for free
confirmed.symptom: A player who follows the game's own on-screen instruction ('Click / tap left or right half to drop a weight... keep the beam balanced for 60 seconds') places one single, ordinary weight and, within about one second, watches the beam repeatedly slam past the tilt limit and lose all 3 lives in rapid succession -- with no second falling weight even having spawned yet to attempt a correction. Meanwhile a player who never clicks at all reaches the 'Balanced!' win screen automatically at 60 seconds.
confirmed.rootCause: update() (index.html lines 122-126) has no restoring torque proportional to -angle -- only weights.reduce(...) torque plus a 0.98 velocity damping term. Weights are never removed from the beam once placed (weights array only grows, cleared solely on resetGame). So any nonzero net sum of placed weights' (val*dx) is a permanent forcing term: angleV decays toward a NONZERO steady state (not toward 0), meaning angle grows monotonically forever once any imbalance exists, until it exceeds MAX_ANGLE (0.28) at line 133 and the tip-over penalty fires -- repeatedly, because the same weight is still on the beam next frame. Compounding this, placeFalling() (lines 77-79) injects an immediate one-shot kick (torque = val*dx*0.00015, up to 0.03 rad/frame) directly into angleV with no dt scaling, which by itself is enough to start the runaway climb toward MAX_ANGLE within ~15-16 frames for a typical placement.
confirmed.evidence: Placed exactly one weight (val=1, non-heavy) and drove update(16) with nothing else touched, across 10 different seeds giving dx values from -70 to -199 (the game's actual random range is roughly ±40 to ±202): all 10/10 reached state='gameover' with lives=0 in 64-78 frames (~1.0-1.3 simulated seconds) -- e.g. seed=1: dx=-121, angle climbed 0->0.28 by frame 16, then re-tipped at frames 58 and 68, losing all 3 lives before the next weight (spawn interval starts at 2800ms) could ever arrive. A 25-seed sweep using an 'omniscient' strategy that always chooses the side minimizing the running net torque sum of every weight ever placed still produced 0/25 wins; best survived time across all 25 trials was 1.25s out of the 60s required. Control: g.resetGame(false) followed by driving update(16) with ZERO placeFalling calls ever -> game.state ends as 'won', game.survived=60.0, game.score=0.

confirmed.title: Missing shot-counted re-entry guard in the out-of-bounds miss branch of update()
confirmed.symptom: Miss even one shot, and the "Shot X / 20" counter races upward by itself over the next fraction of a second without any further input, burning through most or all of the remaining round (in the playthrough test the round reached the 'won' screen after only 2 flicks) — the player never gets their advertised 20 shots.
confirmed.rootCause: In update(), the made-shot branch (lines ~116-145) is guarded by a `scored` flag that only resets on the next flick(), so a make can only be counted once. The out-of-bounds miss branch (`if(ball.x<-40||ball.x>W+40||ball.y>H+40){...game.shots++;...}`, lines ~147-160) has no equivalent guard. Once the ball goes out of bounds, `ballInFlight` stays true and `ball` stays non-null until the scheduled `setTimeout(...,500)` finally fires to clear them; meanwhile the ball keeps moving further out of bounds each frame, so the same `if` condition re-fires and re-increments `game.shots` (and re-calls `endShotIfDone()`, re-triggering `sndWin()`/`saveBest()` once the total is passed) on every single subsequent update() call until that reset timeout lands.
confirmed.evidence: resetGame() -> flick(-20,-140) (a flick that falls straight down, never near the hoop, so it is unambiguously a miss) -> pumping update(16) for ~1.28s at real frame cadence (yielding to the event loop each frame, replicating how rAF and setTimeout actually interleave in a browser) took game.shots from 0 to 17 (expected: 0 -> 1). In the exhaust-the-round test, the game reached the 'won' screen after only 2 flick() calls even though g.TOTAL === 20. Control check: forcing an actual make (ball placed dead-center under the hoop, vy=500) left game.shots and game.score completely unchanged across 5 further update(16) calls, confirming the `scored` guard on the make path works correctly and the bug is specific to the unguarded miss path.
confirmed.fix:
Add a `missed` re-entry guard mirroring the existing `scored` guard.

1. Line 49: `let scored=false,missAnim=0;` -> `let scored=false,missed=false,missAnim=0;`
2. Line 66 (resetGame): `...scored=false;missAnim=0;` -> `...scored=false;missed=false;missAnim=0;`
3. Line 92 (flick): `ballInFlight=true;scored=false;missAnim=0;sndThrow();` -> `ballInFlight=true;scored=false;missed=false;missAnim=0;sndThrow();`
4. Line 147: `if(ball.x<-40||ball.x>W+40||ball.y>H+40){` -> `if((ball.x<-40||ball.x>W+40||ball.y>H+40)&&!missed){ missed=true;` (keep the rest of the branch unchanged)

CRITICAL — the obvious fix leaves a second path broken: clearing `missed` inside the 500ms reset setTimeout (the natural-looking place, next to `ball=null;ballInFlight=false;`) is WRONG. That timeout is never scheduled on the final shot, because endShotIfDone() returns true when game.shots >= TOTAL and the `if(!done)` block is skipped. A fix that only clears the flag there would fix the mid-round counter but leave the last-shot/win path still re-firing sndWin() and saveBest() every frame for 600ms. The flag must be cleared in flick() — the only place that sets ballInFlight=true — exactly as `scored` already is (and in resetGame() for hygiene).

Alternative one-line minimal fix: reuse the existing, already-correctly-managed `scored` flag by changing line 147 to `if((ball.x<-40||ball.x>W+40||ball.y>H+40)&&!scored){ scored=true; ...`. `scored` is only read at lines 117/118 and cleared at 66/92, and setting it on an out-of-bounds ball cannot suppress a legitimate make (the ball is already off-screen), so this is functionally safe — but the dedicated `missed` flag is clearer.

confirmed.title: Pausing during the inter-wave gap permanently freezes wave progression
confirmed.symptom: Player taps the pause button (top-right control) at the exact moment the game invites it - right after a wave clears, during the on-screen 'Wave clear' banner, with zero enemies on screen. Unpausing does nothing: no more enemies ever spawn, the wave counter never advances again, no error appears. The run is stuck forever in a live 'playing' state with nothing happening.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:363-371. When a wave clears, the game sets game.spawning=false and schedules `setTimeout(()=>{ if(game.state==='playing') startWave(game.wave+1); },2000)`. If the player pauses during that 2-second real-time window, the callback's `game.state==='playing'` check is false when it fires, so it silently no-ops. Nothing else in the codebase ever retries or reschedules that call - resetGame() is the only other caller of startWave(). Since game.spawning is already false, the wave-clear branch inside update() (line 363) can never re-trigger either. The transition opportunity is simply gone.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced in test_boundary-tower.js ('power/utility path: pausing during the inter-wave gap'). After wave 1 resolves (game.wave===1, game.spawning===false), togglePause() -> state 'paused' -> real sleep(2300ms) (the internal 2000ms timer elapses while paused) -> togglePause() -> state 'playing' -> pump 20000ms more of simulated play (update() called ~1200 times at dt=16.67). Expected: game.wave becomes 2 (as it does in an unpaused control run of the identical scenario, confirmed passing in the same suite). Actual: game.wave stays 1 forever, game.spawning stays false, enemies stays []. Verified this is not a harness artifact by patching a throwaway copy of the game (driving the 2000ms delay from update()'s own dt via a pendingNextWaveT counter instead of a raw setTimeout, so pausing correctly suspends and resumes it) and rerunning the identical suite: the assertion flips from FAIL to ok on that fixed copy.
confirmed.fix:
Remove the raw real-time setTimeout and drive the delay from update()'s own dt, so pausing suspends and resumes it.

1) Add a field to the `game` object (line 132): `nextWaveT:0,`
2) Replace line 370 `setTimeout(()=>{ if(game.state==='playing') startWave(game.wave+1); },2000);` with `game.nextWaveT=2000;`
3) In update(), immediately AFTER the `if(game.state!=='playing') return;` guard (line 347) so it only ticks while playing:
   `if(game.nextWaveT>0){ game.nextWaveT-=dt; if(game.nextWaveT<=0){ game.nextWaveT=0; startWave(game.wave+1); } }`
4) Add `game.nextWaveT=0;` to resetGame() (near line 169).

Verified: this exact patch on a throwaway copy flips the failing assertion to ok while the unpaused control still passes.

EXPLICIT WARNING — the obvious alternative fix leaves a SECOND path broken. Retrying the transition inside togglePause() on resume while KEEPING the setTimeout is worse than the bug. I built and ran it: a brief 300ms pause inside the gap resumes -> startWave(2), then the still-pending original 2000ms timeout fires -> startWave(3), SILENTLY SKIPPING WAVE 2 (observed wave: 2 then wave: 3). The raw setTimeout must be deleted, not compensated for.

On step 4 specifically, be accurate: the resetGame() clear is defensive hygiene rather than a currently-live second path, because nextWaveT>0 only while enemies.length===0, so no life can be lost and no gameover can occur during the countdown — and resetGame() is only reachable from the title/gameover states. It costs nothing and protects the invariant if the gap ever gains enemies, so include it.

confirmed.title: Towers make no measurable difference to survival at the game's own starting budget
confirmed.symptom: A player who places towers with their starting 100 gold (the maximum the intended economy allows before wave 1 even begins) loses exactly the same number of lives and ends with exactly the same score as a player who places no towers at all. The stated core mechanic - 'Click empty grid squares to place boundary towers... Stop pressures before they breach your space' - has zero effect at the only budget level normal play ever reaches.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:265-284 (towersUpdate) + 297-321 (projUpdate). Each tower's fire rate (800-1800ms per shot, TOWER_TYPES) is far slower than the time any single enemy spends inside that tower's range circle (~200ms, derived from range 2.0-3.5 tiles * CELL(36px) vs. enemy speed 0.34-1.46 px/ms) because the path only passes each grid cell once - so a lone tower gets at most ~1 shot per enemy during its one transit, when killing a 60hp wave-1 enemy needs 4-8 hits. Confirmed this isn't a projectile-speed artifact: even patching a throwaway copy with fire-rate floored to 120ms (12x faster) AND projectile homing speed raised 8x (5->40) together, 2 towers at the path-start chokepoint still landed exactly one 8-damage hit total across the whole wave and killed nothing. Killing anything at all requires many towers lining most of the path (verified: ~114 towers, paid for with cheat gold, cut wave-1 losses from 8 lives to 4) - far beyond the 2 towers the starting 100 gold buys, and gold income beyond the flat wave-clear bonus depends entirely on kills that essentially never happen at that budget.
confirmed.evidence: test_boundary-tower.js 'core loop: does a placed tower actually change the outcome vs. no defense?': same deterministic wave-1 seed (seeded(wave*1337+7)) in both runs. No-tower run: lives 10->2, score 24 (pure wave-clear bonus, 20+wave*4, no kill component). With-tower run (2 towers placed at the path-start chokepoint, all 100 starting gold spent): lives 10->2, score 24 - byte-for-byte identical. Reproduced consistently across repeated suite runs. Separately confirmed a stationary target 36px from the same tower DOES take damage correctly under realistic dt (60->52->44 over successive cooldown cycles), ruling out a general hit-detection failure - the problem is specific to the mismatch between fire rate and how briefly a moving enemy dwells in any one tower's range.
confirmed.fix:
Two changes are required; either one alone leaves the game equally broken (I verified each in isolation against a full playthrough — projectile speed alone: gameover wave 2; fire rate alone: gameover wave 2; enemy speed /8, /10 or /12 alone: STILL gameover wave 2 score 24, because the nearest-target retargeting in towersUpdate spreads fire across the slowed pack so nothing crosses the kill threshold).

1. index.html:239 (moveEnemy) — fix the cells-per-frame vs px-per-frame unit mismatch:
     const spd=e.type.speed*(dt/16)/10;
   (equivalently, divide every PRESSURES[].speed and BOSS_PRESSURE.speed by 10)

2. index.html:123-125 (TOWER_TYPES) — triple tower damage so DPS matches dwell time:
     Boundary damage 8 -> 24
     Clarity  damage 18 -> 54
     Calm     damage 6 -> 18

Verified on a patched throwaway copy (never the audited file), full playthrough with greedy reinvestment:
  baseline          -> wave 2 | lives 0  | score 24   | 2 towers  | gameover
  speed/10 only     -> wave 2 | lives 0  | score 24   | 2 towers  | gameover
  speed/10 + dmg x3 -> wave 8 | lives 10 | score 2618 | 39 towers | playing

The fixed build snowballs correctly: the 2 starting towers get kills on wave 1, kills fund more towers, and the economy compounds. No change to the gold economy itself is needed once kills are possible.

confirmed.title: Completing any frame (2nd ball, or a strike, or reaching frame 10) leaves the ball 'live' forever, so update() re-fires endBall()/endFrame() every single animation tick until the game ends itself after 1-2 balls
confirmed.symptom: The player throws exactly two balls of frame 1 (or one strike) and within about a second the frame counter and pin/score readouts race on their own, and the whole 10-frame game abruptly ends (gameover or won) -- no matter how the player actually bowled. Frames 2 through 9 are never played; input to bowl a 3rd ball is refused because the game is already over.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:146-165 endBall(). The only branch that resets `ball=null;ballMoving=false` is line 154 (ball 1 of an OPEN, non-final frame). Every other branch -- the entire ball-2 `else` at lines 155-164, and the ball-1 `if(knocked===10||game.frame>=TOTAL_FRAMES)` branch at line 153 -- calls endFrame() but never clears `ball`/`ballMoving`. Meanwhile update() (line 112 `if(!ballMoving)return;`, line 142-143 `const stalled=...; if(...||stalled){endBall();}`) keeps running every tick while ballMoving stays true, and the stalled ball's residual velocity is never zeroed, so `stalled` stays true forever. Each subsequent tick re-invokes endBall(), which for the ball-2 branch mutates the same frameLog entry and unconditionally calls endFrame() again; endFrame()'s non-final branch (line 204) does a bare `game.frame++` with no guard against having already advanced this same physical roll, so frame free-runs by +1 every ~16ms tick until it hits TOTAL_FRAMES and the final-frame branch (lines 193-202) schedules game.state='gameover'/'won' 700/800ms later.
confirmed.evidence: Deterministic gutter run (0 pins both balls, no RNG involved): after ball 1, frame=1, ball1=false, frameLog=[{k1:0,k2:0}], game.pins=0 (correct). Released ball 2, then ran 80 update(16) ticks: game.frame read 10 (expected 2) while frameLog.length stayed 1 and game.pins stayed 0 the whole time -- i.e. only one real frame was ever played but the frame counter had already reached the end. Granular tick trace showed frame incrementing by exactly +1 on ticks 51 through 59 (one per update() call, 16ms apart) immediately after ball 2 settled. After `await sleep(900)` (letting the real setTimeout delays elapse), game.state became 'gameover' with frameLog.length still 1 -- the entire game ended after 2 balls. A subsequent releaseBall() call had no effect (state!=='playing'). Repeated with a center-aimed (non-gutter) first ball that actually knocked down pins: same result -- game.frame raced to 10 with frameLog.length still 1. resetGame() (the game's real restart handler, called from title/won/gameover clicks) does cleanly restore frame=1/state=playing/frameLog=[]/ball=null, and a fresh game exhibits the identical bug on its own second ball -- so restart itself is not broken, but the game is unplayable past its first frame every time.

confirmed.title: Cave 6 of 10 is unconditionally unbeatable: the level spawns the player already overlapping the tunnel wall, with zero reaction time, and the identical fatal geometry regenerates on every retry
confirmed.symptom: A player who has cleared caves 1-5 flawlessly reaches cave 6 (game.round===5) and is instantly hit on the very first rendered frame, before any keypress could possibly matter. Retrying (the game's own hit-recovery path, buildLevel(game.round)) reproduces the exact same instant hit again, and again, burning all 3 lives in 3 frames and ending the run at 'Cave 6 / 10' every single time. The advertised '10 caves' premise (title screen, HUD, hint text) is not reachable past cave 6 in this shipped file.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:143 pins player.x=80 for the whole game, and SEG_W=80 (line 50), so the very first collision check of any level (game.scroll≈0) already resolves segAt(worldX) to segment index 1, not index 0 (line 152, segAt at line 112) — segment 0 is drawn but never actually collided against. buildLevel() (lines 102-111) always resets the player to a fixed spawn y=H/2=270 regardless of where that real first-checked segment has drifted to. buildSegs() (lines 82-101) generates the tunnel centreline as a seeded random walk starting from H/2, so by the time segment index 1 is generated (two random-walk steps in) its cy can already be far from 270; combined with the gap shrinking every round (line 84: minGap=180-r*8), the player's fixed spawn point can already violate the wall (line 155's check) before update() has moved it at all — a single simulation frame only shifts y by ~0.08px from rest (vy starts at 0, line 108), nowhere near enough to close a multi-pixel violation. Because buildSegs(r) is seeded only by the round number (line 83: seeded(r*6007+41)), this exact geometry — and therefore this exact spawn violation — reproduces byte-for-byte on every retry of the same round (the hit-handler's buildLevel(game.round) at line 169 passes the same r every time).
confirmed.evidence: Reached via legitimate bot-driven play (resetGame() then clearing rounds 0,1,2,3,4 through the real win path) — no state was forced. At round 5 (Cave 6/10): player spawn yStart=270.0 (PLAYER_R=12), and the actually-collision-checked segment is segs[1] with cy=337.4, gap=140, giving topWall=267.4. marginTop = (270-12)-267.4 = -9.4, i.e. the player already overlaps the top wall by 9.4px at frame 0 — confirmed even when the objectively correct input direction is held from t=0 (best-case probe, still 'FAIL' in the test output). A single g.update(16) call from this fresh state costs a life every time; replaying the round from scratch 5 more times to confirm determinism showed 3 more identical hits cascading lives 3->2->1->0 in 3 frames flat, landing in 'roundover-wait' then 'gameover'. Full test output line: 'round 5 spawn: yStart=270.0 seg1.cy=337.4 seg1.gap=140 topWall=267.4 botWall=407.4 marginTop=-9.4 marginBot=125.4' followed by 'FAIL cave 6's spawn position does not already overlap the first checked wall segment'.

confirmed.title: dropGems() never re-checks solvability, so legitimate cascades can leave a board with zero valid moves for the rest of the round
confirmed.symptom: Mid-round, after making only ordinary matches (following the game's own hint suggestions, nothing exotic), every gem on the board becomes unswappable: clicking any adjacent pair produces no match, no matter which pair is tried. The player has 40+ seconds still on the clock and a score far short of the round target, but can do nothing productive - the round is only resolved by watching the timer run out to 'timesup'.
confirmed.rootCause: buildGrid() (index.html:80-94) explicitly calls ensureSolvable() after generating the initial board, guaranteeing at least one valid swap exists at round start. But dropGems() (index.html:234-242), which refills the board with fresh random gems after every match and every cascade step, has no equivalent call - swapGems()'s cascade loop (index.html:244-266) repeatedly calls removeMatches()+dropGems() with no solvability guard in between. A sufficiently unlucky refill during a cascade can leave the entire grid with no 3-in-a-row possible from any adjacent swap, and nothing in update() or the hint system (findHintMove()/requestHint()) detects or fixes this - hasAnyValidMove() simply returns false and stays false.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced standalone via window.__cfq: g.startRound(15); then repeatedly playing the move g.findHintMove() suggests. After exactly 21 legitimate moves, g.hasAnyValidMove() flips to false with g.game.score=93 (target g.roundTarget(15)=1420) and g.game.roundTimer=44664 of the round's 45000ms budget still remaining. 500 further g.update(16) calls with zero input leave state 'playing' and hasAnyValidMove() still false - the board never self-recovers; only the timer eventually resolves it into 'timesup'. This is deterministic and reproducible every run because rng=makeRng(round) reseeds per round number. A broader sweep across rounds 1-40 (greedy hint-following) hit the same dead-board condition 3 times (rounds 15, 32, 39), each time with well over half the round's time still on the clock.
confirmed.fix:
In swapGems() (index.html:244-266), call ensureSolvable() once after the cascade loop has settled - i.e. immediately after line 253, before `game.combo=mult;`:

    while(more.size>0){ mult++; removeMatches(more,mult); dropGems(); more=findMatches(); }
    ensureSolvable();          // <-- add: board has settled, guarantee a move exists
    game.combo=mult;

IMPORTANT - the obvious fix implied by the report's title is WRONG and would leave a second path broken: do NOT put ensureSolvable() inside dropGems(). dropGems() is called at line 250 and again inside the loop at line 253 while findMatches() is still non-empty; reshuffling there would destroy the pending cascade matches (silently eating the player's combo) and ensureSolvable()'s trial swaps would be evaluating a board that still has standing matches, so hasAnyValidMove() would return true for the wrong reason. The check must run only after the cascade fully settles (more.size===0).

Coverage is complete at that single point: dropGems() and removeMatches() have no callers other than swapGems() lines 250/253, and buildGrid() - the only other code path that mutates the whole board - already calls ensureSolvable() at line 93. Special-gem expansion (expandSpecials) also resolves through the same cascade loop, so it is covered too.

Caveat: ensureSolvable() rebuilds the entire grid from fresh newGem()s, so a rescue reshuffle wipes any special (line/color) gems currently on the board. That is functionally correct and acceptable for a minimal fix, but a gem-preserving shuffle (permute existing gems, retry until hasAnyValidMove() && no pre-existing match) would be a better follow-up if special-gem loss matters.

confirmed.title: A correct answer is double-matched and silently skips the next prompt
confirmed.symptom: The player types the calm response exactly right (matching the greyed-out target text on screen), the score/streak tick up, but the very next round the counter shows they've skipped ahead -- the '10 prompts this round' actually ends after only 5 typed answers, and every second guilt-trip prompt is never shown or answerable.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html checkTyped() (around line 144-162) normalizes both current.r and typed by stripping every character that is not a-z, 0-9, space, or apostrophe: `target=current.r.toLowerCase().replace(/[^a-z0-9 ']/g,'')`. Every single response in PROMPTS (all 12) ends with a period. Because the period is stripped from both sides of the comparison, `t===target` already becomes true the instant the player types everything except the final period -- one keystroke before the sentence is actually finished. checkTyped() is invoked from the #ti 'input' listener on every keystroke (line 457-474), so it matches once at length-1 (missing only the period) and then matches again, independently, when the actual final character (the period) is typed. Each match runs the full 'correct answer' branch: game.score+=gained, game.streak++, and setTimeout(()=>nextPrompt(),350) (line 160). Two independent 350ms timers therefore fire for one completed sentence, and nextPrompt() (line 126-132) unconditionally does `current=queue[game.idx]; game.idx++` on each call -- so the second, spurious call advances past an entire prompt the player never saw, in addition to double-awarding score and streak for a single answer.
confirmed.evidence: Isolated jsdom probe (fresh round, single response 'I'm living the life I want.', typed one real keystroke at a time with a 20-90ms human-paced gap between characters, well under the 350ms timer): after typing every character except the final '.', game.streak was already 1 and game.score already >0. After typing the final '.', game.streak became 2 (expected 1) and game.idx had advanced to 3 from a starting value of 1 (expected 2) once both scheduled nextPrompt() calls settled. Re-run with the game's own real per-frame update() loop for the timeout/lives path (no typing involved) reached gameover cleanly and exactly as expected, confirming the defect is specific to checkTyped()'s trailing-punctuation handling and not a general harness artifact. Driving a full round via typeSlow() (10 real, correct, human-paced answers expected) reached 'roundover' after only 5 typed answers every time.

confirmed.title: The advertised "par bonus" (finish in <=14 moves) is both geometrically unreachable and non-functional dead code
confirmed.symptom: The title screen promises: "Reach the goal in 14 moves or fewer for a bonus." No player can ever earn this bonus, on any of the 10 levels, and even in a hypothetical best case the bonus would not actually add to the score.
confirmed.rootCause: PAR_MOVES=14 (line 44), but player.col is hardcoded to 0 and goal.col is hardcoded to COLS-1=17 for every level (lines 63-66), and goal.row is forced to differ from player.row. So the minimum possible Manhattan distance on every single level is at least 17+1=18 moves, already exceeding PAR_MOVES before accounting for cloud avoidance. Separately, in movePlayer (lines 109-111), `let bonus=0; if(game.moves<=PAR_MOVES){bonus+=1; spawnPopup(...'+1 par bonus!'...)}` computes and discards a local `bonus` variable that is never added to `game.score` or any other field -- grepping the whole file confirms `bonus` appears only on these two lines. The popup fires (falsely implying a reward) but nothing is actually awarded.
confirmed.evidence: Test computed the real shortest safe-path length for rounds 0-9 from the hook's own generated player/goal/clouds: manhattan distances were [20,18,23,18,21,18,21,18,21,18] for rounds 0-9 respectively -- every one > PAR_MOVES=14 (test: 'every level's minimum straight-line distance ... already exceeds PAR_MOVES'). Independently, placing the player one step from the goal with game.moves=1 and taking the winning step (moves becomes 2, well under 14) produced game.score increasing by exactly 1 (from 0 to 1), not 2, even though the moves<=PAR_MOVES branch executed and spawned the '+1 par bonus!' popup -- proving the bonus is dead code regardless of reachability.
confirmed.fix:
Two independent defects — fixing either alone leaves the feature broken, and the OBVIOUS fix to the second one breaks a THIRD path. Minimal correct fix:

1) Make par attainable. Line 44: change `const PAR_MOVES=14;` to `const PAR_MOVES=22;`. The structural floor is 18 (17 columns + at least 1 row), and cloud detours typically add a few, so 22 is earnable by efficient play but not automatic. (Alternative, if 14 must be kept: stop hardcoding the endpoints in buildLevel — but that is a larger, riskier change to level design.)

2) Award the bonus — but NOT into game.score. `game.score` doubles as the level/progress counter: the 'won' panel renders 'Score: '+game.score+' / '+TOTAL and 'Clean levels: '+game.cleanLevels+' / '+game.score (lines 340-342), the HUD renders 'Score '+game.score (line 299), and saveBest() persists it to localStorage 'cw_best' (lines 91-93). So the naive `game.score+=bonus` would render "Score: 16 / 10", corrupt the clean-levels ratio, and poison the saved best — a second code path still broken. Instead add a separate counter:
   - line ~53 in the game object: add `bonusPoints:0,`
   - in resetGame() (line 86-87): add `game.bonusPoints=0;`
   - lines 110-111: replace `let bonus=0;` / `if(game.moves<=PAR_MOVES){bonus+=1;spawnPopup(...)}` with:
     `if(game.moves<=PAR_MOVES){game.bonusPoints++;spawnPopup(GX+goal.col*CELL+CELL/2,GY+goal.row*CELL+CELL/2-10,'+1 par bonus!','255,214,120');}`
   - surface it so the reward is visible: add `'Par bonuses: '+game.bonusPoints,` to the 'won' panel lines (line 340) and the 'gameover' panel lines (line 342), and optionally to drawHUD.

3) Keep the title-screen text truthful: line 338 already interpolates PAR_MOVES ('Reach the goal in '+PAR_MOVES+' moves or fewer for a bonus.'), so it updates automatically with step 1 — no edit needed there, but verify it reads sensibly after the change.

confirmed.title: undoLastDrop() does not cancel the pending AI-reply timeout, letting the AI move (twice) with no player input
confirmed.symptom: Player drops a disc, then immediately uses the Undo button/key before the AI visually replies. The board reverts to empty and it looks like the player's turn again — but a moment later, with zero further input from the player, an AI disc appears on the board, then a SECOND AI disc appears right after it, before control finally returns to the player.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html: startDrop() arms `setTimeout(()=>{ if(game.state==='playing') aiMove(); },300)` (line ~216) when turn passes to AI. undoLastDrop() (line ~164) reverts `grid` and `game.turn` but never cancels this timeout and has no way to (it isn't stored). When the stale timeout fires, its only guard is `game.state==='playing'`, not whose turn it is, so aiMove()/startDrop(AI) runs unconditionally. Then update()'s turn-toggle `game.turn=game.turn===PLAYER?AI:PLAYER` (line ~213) reads the reverted `game.turn` (PLAYER) instead of tracking who actually just moved, so it flips back to AI and re-arms a second AI-move timeout, producing a second uncontested AI drop before the turn finally settles on PLAYER.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced with real (non-mocked) timers: resetGame(); clickCol(col0); pump(60) -> filled=1, turn=AI. undoLastDrop() -> filled=0, turn=PLAYER (board looks pristine, player's turn). await 450ms; pump(60) -> filled=1, turn=AI (an AI disc appeared with no player move). await 450ms; pump(60) -> filled=2, turn=PLAYER (a second AI disc landed). Final grid bottom row: [2,2,0,0,0,0,0] — two AI discs, zero player discs, after the player's only action was to click Undo.

confirmed.title: Extra click during the post-completion window throws an uncaught TypeError
confirmed.symptom: No visible in-game glitch for most players (state/score are untouched), but an extra tap on any star in the roughly 600ms window right after finishing a constellation - before the scheduled 'advance to next pattern / win' timer fires - throws an uncaught exception, visible as a console error and a broken input event.
confirmed.rootCause: clickStar() (line 77-79) always reads `pattern.pts[clicked.length]` to find the next expected point. Once a pattern is fully solved, clicked.length equals pattern.pts.length, but phase does not flip out of 'input' until the setTimeout scheduled at completion (~line 97-104) fires 600ms later. Any clickStar() call in that window indexes pattern.pts out of bounds, getting `undefined`, which is then passed to patternStarIdx(pt) (line 67: `stars.findIndex(s=>s.c===pt[0]&&s.r===pt[1])`), dereferencing `undefined[0]` and throwing.
confirmed.evidence: Directly reproduced: after solveCurrentPattern() finished all 6 points of pattern 0 (g.clicked.length===6===g.pattern.pts.length, g.game.phase still 'input'), calling g.clickStar(0) synchronously threw "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading '0')". Confirmed this is reachable via the real input path: pointerDown() only blocks clicks when state!=='playing', phase!=='input', or paused - none hold during this window - so a real extra mousedown on any star hits clickStar() with a valid index and throws the same way. No lasting damage was observed: clicked.length is unchanged after the throw (the exception fires before any push), the pending 600ms transition still completes normally afterward (verified state settled to 'playing' or 'won' as expected), and the round is not soft-locked.

confirmed.title: The 60% win condition is unreachable under every tested play strategy — the game cannot be won
confirmed.symptom: A player plants all 8 available crystal seeds, watches every one branch and grow to its full size, and the game always ends on the 'Garden Stalled' (gameover) screen. The 'Crystal Garden!' win screen never appears, no matter how or when the seeds are placed.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html caps each crystal at 12 branches (`c.branches.length<12` in update(), and each branch's length is bounded by a finite `b.maxLen` of roughly 40-160px depending on roundsWon (set in plantCrystal()/addBranch())), with only MAX_SEEDS=8 crystals ever allowed. The total stroke geometry 8 fully-grown crystals can ever produce covers only a few percent of the 960x540 canvas, but the win check in calcCoverage() requires `coverage>=TARGET_FILL` where TARGET_FILL=0.60 — needing 311,040 of the canvas's 518,400 pixels lit, over 10x more than the growth model can produce.
confirmed.evidence: 3 independent full-playthrough trials (differing RNG seeds since `seeded()` is keyed off Date.now()): peak coverage 2.58%, 3.14%, 2.82%, every one terminating in 'gameover' at t=9.0s (the STALL_LIMIT). The most generous strategy found — planting only 7 seeds and letting them mature for 60 simulated seconds (the stall clock cannot run below MAX_SEEDS, so this buys unlimited pre-growth time), then planting the 8th seed — still peaked at only 4.56% coverage before ending in gameover 9s later. Best case observed: ~23,700 lit pixels vs the ~311,040 needed to win.
confirmed.fix:
The growth model and the 60% target are ~20x apart, so exactly one of the two must move. Buffing growth alone does NOT close it (verified: an aggressive patch still peaked at 19%). Meet in the middle, verified reachable:

1. Scale the growth model in index.html:
   - update() line 110: `c.branches.length<12` -> `c.branches.length<60`
   - plantCrystal() line 70: `maxLen:40+rng()*60,speed:30+rng()*20` -> `maxLen:70+rng()*90,speed:60+rng()*40`
   - addBranch() line 81: branch from a random existing branch's TIP rather than always from the crystal origin (store per-branch `ox`/`oy` offsets), with `maxLen:50+rng()*70`. This is what turns a starburst into area-filling growth.
2. Set `TARGET_FILL=0.10` (line 36). With the above I measured peaks of 11.26% and 19.05% across trials, so 0.10 is reachable; 0.60 is not reachable under any tuning that keeps the crystals looking like crystals.

TWO SECOND CODE PATHS the obvious fix leaves broken:
(a) index.html:24 -- the #hint div HARDCODES the string "60%" ("grow your garden to fill 60% of the space"). Changing TARGET_FILL updates drawHUD(), the title panel, and the gameover panel (all compute from the constant) but leaves this hint text stale and lying to the player. Must be updated by hand (or made to read from TARGET_FILL).
(b) drawCrystalOC() (line 154: lineWidth=3, ONE facet) and drawCrystal() (line 170: lineWidth=2.5, TWO facets) are two independent geometry paths. Only drawCrystalOC feeds calcCoverage(). If a fix thickens the offscreen stroke to inflate coverage without matching the on-screen stroke, the HUD percentage will diverge from what the player actually sees drawn. Any stroke-width change must be applied to both.

(Also note: difficulty()'s radBoost grows void radii with roundsWon while growth never scales, so each round after a win is strictly harder -- worth capping if wins become possible.)

confirmed.title: Ring-priority loop makes every on-board hit score the same flat 5 points; the 50/30/20/10-point inner rings are mathematically unreachable
confirmed.symptom: A player who lands a dart dead-center in the bullseye (the 'Clarity' ring, visually labelled +50 on the scoreboard) is awarded only 5 points and the popup/label reads 'Awareness' -- the outermost, lowest-value ring. This is true at every distance from the centre: 0px, 20px, 50px, 90px, and 140px from centre (which should score 50, 50, 30, 20, and 10 respectively) all scored exactly 5. Only two outcomes are actually distinguishable during play: hit-anywhere-on-the-board (flat 5pts) or miss (0pts). The distinct rings, colors, and per-ring point labels drawn on the board are cosmetic only -- precision aiming has zero effect on score.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:125-126, inside throwDart(): `for(let i=RINGS.length-1;i>=0;i--){ if(d<=RINGS[i].r){pts=RINGS[i].pts;label=RINGS[i].label;ringIdx=i;break;} }`. RINGS is ordered smallest-radius-first (index 0 = Clarity r=30/50pts ... index 4 = Awareness r=200/5pts). The loop starts at i=4 (the LARGEST radius, Awareness) and `break`s on the first match. Since d<=200 is true for virtually any on-board hit, the loop always matches Awareness on its very first iteration and never reaches the smaller, higher-value rings nested inside it. The loop needed to check from the smallest radius outward (i=0 upward) to give the innermost ring priority, or use exclusive per-band bounds instead of `<=` against a monotonically increasing radius list checked outside-in.
confirmed.evidence: Deterministic reproduction (win.Math.random forced to 0.5, collapsing wobble to 0) at controlled distances from (CX,CY)=(560,270): d=0 -> expected 50/Clarity, got 5/Awareness. d=20 (inside r=30 Clarity) -> expected 50, got 5. d=50 (inside r=60 Focus) -> expected 30, got 5. d=90 (inside r=100 Purpose) -> expected 20, got 5. d=140 (inside r=150 Intention) -> expected 10, got 5. d=190 (inside r=200 Awareness) -> correctly 5. d=210 (outside r=200) -> correctly 0/Miss. All 5 failing cases are in tests/test_darts-clarity.js under 'logic: inner rings must award more than outer rings'.

confirmed.title: Streak/combo bonus system can never trigger, as a direct consequence of the ring bug above
confirmed.symptom: The scoreboard's 'Streak: N (x bonus)' readout and the streak-based +5-per-consecutive-hit bonus never activate, no matter how many bullseyes are hit in a row. A player who hits dead-center three times running still shows Streak: 0 the whole time.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:128: `const isHighValue=ringIdx>=0&&ringIdx<=STREAK_RING_IDX;` (STREAK_RING_IDX=1, meaning only Clarity or Focus should count). Because the ring-detection loop above can never return ringIdx 0 or 1 for an on-board hit (it always returns ringIdx=4, Awareness), isHighValue is always false, so game.streak is reset to 0 on every single hit (index.html:137's else-branch) and the combo bonus (index.html:134, 5*(streak-1)) is permanently dead code in practice.
confirmed.evidence: Deterministic repro: resetGame(); win.Math.random forced to 0.5; throwDart(CX,CY) three times in a row (dead-center each time). Expected streak to reach 1, then 3. Actual: game.streak stayed 0 after every single throw. See tests/test_darts-clarity.js 'logic: streak/combo bonus' section.

confirmed.title: Dissolve/streak score bonuses are erased every frame; final score is pure survival time
confirmed.symptom: Clicking echoes shows a floating '+50' (or more with streak bonus) popup, but the on-screen SCORE and the persisted Best never actually reflect any of it. A player who dissolves constantly ends a round with the exact same score as a player who dissolves nothing at all — only survival time matters.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:101-103, inside update(): `game.score=Math.floor(game.survived*5)+game.score*0;` followed by `game.score=Math.max(game.score,Math.floor(game.survived*5));`. `game.score*0` is always 0 regardless of the current value, so this line unconditionally resets score to the time-only floor on every single update() tick (every animation frame in real play), discarding whatever dissolveAt() just added via `game.score+=total` (line 155). The `Math.max` line that follows can only ever raise the score back up to that same time floor, never restore the erased bonus.
confirmed.evidence: Single-tick reproduction: dissolveAt() on a freshly spawned echo raised game.score to 50 (base 20 + full early-bonus 30, streak 1). One subsequent update(16) call dropped it straight to 0 (survived≈0.016s → floor(0.016*5)=0). Full-round reproduction: playing an entire 60s round while dissolving every echo the instant it spawned (3751 successful dissolveAt() calls, each with streak/early-bonus math applying) ended with game.score===300, exactly equal to Math.floor(survived*5)=Math.floor(60.016*5)=300 — identical to what a player who dissolved zero echoes would get. localStorage 'echoDissolve.best' was also saved as 300, so the persisted best score is likewise just elapsed time regardless of skill.
confirmed.fix: Track dissolve bonuses in their own accumulator instead of writing them into a field that update() overwrites. Four edits: (1) game literal, line 46: add `bonus:0`. (2) resetGame, line 54: add `game.bonus=0;`. (3) dissolveAt, line 155: change `game.score+=total;` to `game.bonus+=total;`. (4) Replace lines 101-103 entirely with the single line `game.score=Math.floor(game.survived*5)+game.bonus;`. CAVEAT — the obvious minimal fix leaves a second path broken two ways. (a) Merely deleting the `+game.score*0` reset and relying on the surviving `game.score=Math.max(game.score,Math.floor(game.survived*5));` does preserve bonuses, but yields score=max(sum-of-bonuses, time*5) rather than additive scoring, so early bonuses stay invisible until they exceed the time floor — not the advertised behaviour. (b) Once game.bonus exists there are TWO reset sites: the game object literal at line 46 and resetGame at line 54. resetGame is the only one that runs on restart/endless (it is what onDown and the keydown('e') handler call), so zeroing bonus in the literal alone leaks accumulated bonuses across every round including endless mode. Do not pattern-match off bestStreak, which resetGame deliberately does not clear.

confirmed.title: Advertised 'distractor tile' mechanic is dead code
confirmed.symptom: The title screen tells the player '3 misses ends the run — avoid the flickering distractor tile,' but no tile ever flickers as a distractor and clicking any tile can never trigger the distractor penalty. The game is simpler/safer than advertised, not broken.
confirmed.rootCause: buildRound() (line 65) computes distractorStep/distractorTile per round for round>=4, and a full clickDistractor() penalty function exists (line 109), but nothing in draw()/drawTiles() ever renders a tile as a distractor (no branch reads distractorTile there), and nothing in tileAt()/onDown()/clickTile() ever calls clickDistractor(). It is assigned once and read nowhere.
confirmed.evidence: grep for 'distractorTile' and 'clickDistractor' across the file: distractorTile is only ever set to -1 or a computed-then-unused value in buildRound; clickDistractor has exactly one occurrence in the whole file (its own declaration) — zero call sites. Confirmed at runtime: win.clickDistractor is a function on the page global but is unreachable from any input path exercised through window.__cfq (clickTile/tileAt have no distractor branch).
confirmed.fix:
Minimal fix is to correct the advertising, NOT to implement the mechanic. In C:\Users\admin\ancf-digital-projects\games\echo-match\index.html line 258, change the title-panel line '3 misses ends the run — avoid the flickering distractor tile.' to '3 misses ends the run.' The #hint div (line 24) already says only '3 misses ends the run' with no distractor claim, so no second copy path needs touching. Optionally delete the now-provably-dead distractorTile/distractorStep declarations (line 53), the distractorTile/distractorStep assignments in buildRound (line 65), and clickDistractor() (lines 109-118); this is cosmetic cleanup and carries no behavior change.

EXPLICIT WARNING ABOUT THE OTHER FIX DIRECTION: if someone instead tries to make the advertised mechanic real, the obvious one-line change leaves a second (in fact third) path still broken. buildRound sets distractorTile=-1 UNCONDITIONALLY and only ever computes a step INDEX (distractorStep), never a tile id — so adding a render branch to drawTiles alone would still display nothing. A working feature needs all three: (1) buildRound actually choosing an off-sequence tile id for distractorTile instead of hardcoding -1, (2) a render branch in drawTiles that reads distractorTile, and (3) a dispatch in clickTile/onDown that calls clickDistractor. Additionally clickDistractor duplicates clickTile's miss-handling logic (misses++/flashT/shake/streak reset/MAX_MISSES gameover/replay setTimeout), so the two copies would drift on any future change to the miss rules — they should be factored into one shared registerMiss() helper rather than left parallel.

confirmed.title: Maze generator never opens a corridor cell's forward wall, so the exit is unreachable on every level
confirmed.symptom: The player can move exactly one square away from the spawn point in whichever direction was first carved, then is stuck forever - every other direction bumps a wall. The exit, and almost the entire maze, can never be reached by any input sequence. The game cannot be completed even in principle.
confirmed.rootCause: carveMaze() in index.html lines 97-108 carves an edge between two 'room' cells (r,c) and (nr,nc) that are 2 apart, through a middle 'corridor' cell (mr,mc). Each of the four direction branches (lines 105-108) clears exactly 3 wall flags: the origin cell's wall facing the corridor, the corridor cell's wall facing BACK toward the origin, and the destination cell's wall facing the corridor - but never clears the corridor cell's wall facing FORWARD toward the destination. Since movePlayer() (lines 166-190) only checks the wall of the cell the player is currently standing in (maze[player.r][player.c].bottom/top/left/right), the player can step from the origin into the corridor cell (whose back wall is open) but is blocked leaving it toward the destination (whose forward wall was never cleared). This repeats identically for every edge in the spanning tree, so from the spawn cell only 1 corridor step in any direction is ever traversable.
confirmed.evidence: BFS over the real maze[][] data (same wall logic as movePlayer) from spawn (0,0), run via the game's own buildLevel(lv) for lv=1..6: level 1: 2 of 247 cells reachable, exit reachable=false; level 2: 2 of 247, exit=false; level 3: 2 of 315, exit=false; level 4: 2 of 315, exit=false; level 5: 2 of 391, exit=false; level 6: 2 of 391, exit=false. Manually inspected cells confirm the mechanism: after g.resetGame(), maze[0][0] = {top:true,right:true,bottom:false,left:true} (origin's forward wall open) and maze[1][0] = {top:false,right:true,bottom:true,left:true} (back wall to origin open, but bottom - the wall facing the next room at (2,0) - still true/walled, and it is never cleared by any carve call since (1,0) is a corridor cell, never a 'room' visited by carveMaze itself).

confirmed.title: A round can never be completed once any single firefly is missed — permanent, reproducible soft-lock on the core win condition
confirmed.symptom: The player is guiding their light around, catching fireflies, everything looks normal (collected count going up, HUD reads e.g. 10/12), but the round simply never ends. No crash, no error, no freeze of input — the light still moves, fireflies still blink — but 'Round 1/8' never advances no matter how long the player keeps playing.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html line 58: inside update()'s fly loop, `if(f.collected||f.missed)continue;` permanently skips a fly once `f.missed` is set. A fly is marked missed the moment its own on/off timer flips it from on to off while the player wasn't close enough to catch it in that window (`if(wasOn&&!f.on){f.missed=true;...}`) — it never blinks on again afterward. The round-clear check at line 66, `if(game.collected>=FLIES_PER_ROUND)`, requires literally catching every one of the 12 spawned fireflies (game.collected only increments on an actual catch, line 59). Since two fireflies can be lit simultaneously and the player's single light can only be in one place, at least one is essentially guaranteed to time out uncaught over a round's course — after which that round's maximum reachable `collected` is <=11, so `collected>=12` can never become true again. The only way out is not finishing the round but losing all 3 lives to shadow blobs instead.
confirmed.evidence: Deterministic repro: on a fresh round, never approaching flies[0] at all (chasing every other lit fly instead) — its own blink timer flips it from on to off with the player nowhere near, and the game itself sets ignored.missed=true. Result: game.collected=9, game.round=0, game.state='playing'; running 8,000 more simulated update() ticks changes nothing (game.collected stays 9, game.round stays 0, game.state stays 'playing' the whole time). Separately, with NO deliberate ignoring — a naive bot that simply always chases whatever firefly is currently lit — round 1 alone produced 2 permanently-missed fireflies within the first 32 ticks (collected capped at 10/12, missed=2, round stuck at 0). By contrast, when 12/12 are legitimately collected (fast-forwarded by writing the same paired fields the real catch code writes: `f.collected=true` + `game.collected=12`), `update()` correctly advances `game.round` to 1 and spawns a fresh round — confirming the round-transition logic itself is correct; the bug is specifically that the win condition is very often unreachable during ordinary play.
confirmed.fix:
Minimal one-line fix at index.html line 66 — end the round when no fly is still in play, rather than requiring all 12 to be caught:

  if(game.collected>=FLIES_PER_ROUND){        // before
  if(flies.every(f=>f.collected||f.missed)){  // after

This preserves the existing design (a missed fly leaves the field and costs you score/accuracy) and keeps update()'s and drawFlies()'s `f.collected||f.missed` skips consistent. Verified: patched build clears all 8 rounds and reaches state='won' (Probe E).

SECOND CODE PATH WARNING — the obvious alternative fix is the trap: "let missed flies blink again" (removing `f.missed=true` at line 58, or dropping `||f.missed` from update's skip) leaves drawFlies() at line 83 still doing `if(f.collected||f.missed)continue;`. The fly would keep drifting and remain catchable while being INVISIBLE forever. Anyone taking that route must clear the flag in BOTH line 58 and line 83.

Cosmetic follow-ups (not required for correctness): the 'won' panel line "8 rounds. Every firefly found." becomes inaccurate once rounds can clear with misses, and the HUD "Collected: n/12" will advance the round at e.g. 10/12 — consider showing resolved-count/12 or "Caught: n".

confirmed.title: Restarting during the post-20th-launch pause lets a stale win-timeout later hijack the new round and falsify save stats
confirmed.symptom: Player reaches 20/20 fireworks, then (during the ~1.2s pre-win pause, while the HUD still reads 'all 20 launched — finishing…') taps the always-visible restart button. The game correctly starts a fresh round. The player plays on normally for a bit (e.g. launches 2 more fireworks) -- then, with no further input from the player, the screen is yanked to the 'Celebration Complete!' panel mid-round, and the persisted best-time/completion stats are updated for a round that was never actually finished.
confirmed.rootCause: In launchFirework() (index.html, around the `if(game.count>=TARGET){...setTimeout(()=>{game.state='won';save.completions++;...},1200);}` block), the win transition is scheduled on a bare setTimeout with no id retained. resetGame() (around `function resetGame(){game.state='playing';game.count=0;...}`) has no guard against a pending win-timeout and never cancels one -- there is no clearTimeout anywhere in the file (confirmed via grep). If resetGame() is called after the 20th launch but before the deferred callback fires (a legitimate action: resetBtn's click handler calls resetGame() unconditionally regardless of game.state), the orphaned callback still fires later and unconditionally sets game.state='won', increments save.completions, and records save.fastestMs using the *new* round's elapsed time (game.time - the now-overwritten game.startMs), all while the player's new round is actively in progress.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced live: drove 20 launches to reach game.count===20 (state remained 'playing' as expected). Waited 300ms into the 1200ms pre-win pause, then called resetGame() directly (the real restart handler) -> state correctly went to 'playing', count to 0. Launched 2 more fireworks in the new round -> game.count===2. After the remaining timeout window elapsed (total >1200ms since the original 20th launch), observed: game.state became 'won' (expected to remain 'playing'), and save.completions went from 2 to 3 (expected to stay at 2) -- a completion recorded for a round that had only reached 2 of 20 launches. game.count itself stayed at 2 since the stale callback never touches count, so the resulting 'won' screen would show alongside a round the player could see was unfinished.

confirmed.title: Noise debris is purely decorative — it never affects reel outcome or scoring despite the game's own hint text
confirmed.symptom: The title screen tells the player to 'Avoid the noise debris,' and a code comment claims debris is aimed near the bobber for 'genuine spatial risk,' but a player gains no benefit from avoiding debris and suffers no penalty from a bobber sitting on top of it — the advertised risk/skill element is not implemented.
confirmed.rootCause: doReel() decides its outcome solely from `isNoise=rng()<0.25` (line 109); neither doReel() nor update() ever reads the `debris` array's position when resolving a cast/reel, so debris proximity has zero causal path to the score, fish count, or noise/calm result.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced by running actual code: pushed a synthetic debris object at the bobber's exact (x,y) immediately before calling doReel(), repeated across trials until a noise outcome landed, and the resulting score delta was exactly the fixed -5 noise penalty every time — identical to a noise catch with no debris present, confirming debris presence changes nothing.
confirmed.fix:
MINIMAL CORRECT FIX — change the advertising, not the mechanic. index.html line 383, in the title panelDraw() lines array:
  'Avoid the noise debris.'  ->  'Noise drifts past — reel in only the calm.'
Optionally align the stale comments at :32 ("avoid hooking it") and :186 ("genuine spatial risk") that document the unimplemented mechanic.

EXPLICIT WARNING — THE OBVIOUS FIX LEAVES A SECOND PATH BROKEN. The tempting fix is to add a debris-proximity test in doReel() near :109 (e.g. `const hit=debris.some(d=>Math.abs(d.x-bobber.x)<26&&Math.abs(d.y-bobber.y)<12); const isNoise=hit||rng()<0.25;`). Do NOT ship that alone. Because doCast() (:91-102) picks the bobber position from rng() and handlePrimary() (:398) throws away the click coordinates, collision would punish the player for a position they have no way to influence — turning a harmless cosmetic mismatch into genuine unfairness, and making the outcome MORE random, not more skillful.

A real avoidance mechanic requires BOTH code paths:
  1. doCast() must accept and use the click point — wire up the already-written but never-called canvasPointFromEvent() (:389), pass the point through handlePrimary() (:404) and the keydown handler (:414, which has no pointer event and needs a fallback), and clamp it to the water region (y>HORIZON+15).
  2. Only then add the debris-proximity test in doReel().
That also rebalances the difficulty curve (difficultyDebrisChance() at :76 was tuned for purely decorative debris) and requires re-tuning. Given severity low, the one-line text change is the right call.

confirmed.title: Missing post-sink transition guard lets the hole-complete logic re-fire, silently skipping holes and corrupting the scorecard
confirmed.symptom: After sinking a putt that comes to rest gently in the cup (a normal, in fact game-rewarded, one-putt/'ACE' style shot), the player's very next ordinary putt — or even just the game's own idle frame updates, with zero further input at all — causes the game to silently jump ahead one or more holes. The scorecard and score shown on the eventual 'Course Complete' screen record fabricated entries for holes the player never actually played, and in the worst case the round can finish 'won' with fewer real holes played than 9.
confirmed.rootCause: In update() (index.html, the hole-sunk branch right after the wall-collision loop: `if(dh<HOLE_R&&Math.hypot(ball.vx,ball.vy)<120){...game.hole++;setTimeout(()=>buildHole(game.hole),600);}`), sinking a putt increments game.hole and schedules `buildHole()` 600ms later via setTimeout, but never sets any 'transitioning'/'awaiting next hole' flag and never changes game.state away from 'playing'. For up to 600ms after the sink, the module-level `ball`, `holePos`, and `walls` still reference the JUST-COMPLETED hole, yet player input remains fully live. If the ball's residual speed decays to 0 while still inside the (stale) hole radius — which happens routinely for a soft lag putt, since friction snaps speed to exactly 0 below 4px/s — the same `dh<HOLE_R && speed<120` condition keeps being true on every subsequent update() tick, so the entire hole-complete branch (score bonus, game.hole++, another setTimeout) fires again and again for a hole the player never played, until the original buildHole() timeout finally rebuilds the board.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced twice, deterministically, via window.__cfq: (1) sank hole 9's layout (buildHole(8), wall-free for a clean repro) with a full-power putt(600) then a calibrated soft putt(51.1) that arrives at speed exactly 0 inside the cup (game.hole: 0->1, holeStrokes=[2]). One further, entirely ordinary putt(90) plus a single 16ms update() tick then flipped game.hole 1->2, score 9->17, totalStrokes 2->5, and holeStrokes [2]->[2,3] — a second hole 'completed' and scored without the player ever seeing or playing it. (2) Same setup, but with ZERO extra player input after the sink — just 40 ordinary 16ms update() ticks (640ms of normal frame time, well within the real ~600ms buildHole() delay) — cascaded game.hole from 1 all the way to 6 (holeStrokes ballooning to [2,2,2,2,2,2], score to 54, totalStrokes to 12) purely from the game's own idle loop, no input at all.
confirmed.fix:
Add a one-shot latch cleared by buildHole().

1. index.html:55 — add `sunk:false,` to the `game` object literal.
2. index.html:82 — gate and latch the branch:
   `const dh=Math.hypot(ball.x-holePos.x,ball.y-holePos.y);if(!game.sunk&&dh<HOLE_R&&Math.hypot(ball.vx,ball.vy)<120){game.sunk=true;` (rest of branch unchanged).
3. index.html:59 — clear it inside buildHole: `...game.strokes=0;game.sunk=false;dragStart2=null;game.state='playing';game.paused=false;`

CRITICAL second-path warning: the flag MUST be cleared in buildHole(), NOT in resetGame(). resetGame() delegates to buildHole(0), so clearing in buildHole covers both the hole->hole transition and the won->restart transition. Clearing it in resetGame() instead appears to work (a fresh round starts fine) but leaves the hole-to-hole path permanently latched after the first sink — the player would sink hole 1 and then be unable to complete any further hole for the rest of the round.

Note: the 'won' branch (line 93) intentionally never calls buildHole, so game.sunk stays true there. That is safe — update() returns early at line 78 when state!=='playing', and the restart path (onDown -> resetGame -> buildHole(0)) clears it.

Optional hardening (not required for correctness): the latch alone still leaves player input live during the 600ms transition. A putt in that window bumps game.strokes and gives the stale ball velocity, but buildHole resets both strokes and ball (including vx/vy), so it is cosmetic only.

confirmed.title: Any round with 1-7 misses soft-locks the game forever in 'playing'
confirmed.symptom: The player misses just a star or two during a round (very plausible, especially in round 2/3 where stars move faster and expire sooner). Nothing crashes and no error appears -- the HUD simply stops changing. No new star ever spawns again, clicking anywhere does nothing, and the round/game never ends: no win screen, no game-over screen, nothing. The player is stuck looking at e.g. 'Collected: 17/20 Misses: 3/8' forever with the pause/mute buttons still technically working but the core loop dead.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html line 153's spawn gate `(uncollected+game.collected+game.misses)<PER_ROUND` caps the TOTAL stars ever spawned in a round at exactly PER_ROUND (20) -- once collected+misses reaches 20, nothing spawns again for that round. The only code that actually completes a round is line 185, `if(!roundDone&&game.collected>=PER_ROUND){...}`, which requires FULL collection (collected===20, i.e. zero misses). Lines 180-184 are a dead branch that looks like it was meant to handle the 'ran out of stars without full collection' case -- it has the exact right guard condition (`collected>=PER_ROUND||collected+misses>=PER_ROUND`) but its body is empty except for the comment '// round ran out of stars without reaching target via misses alone; only complete on full collection'. So whenever misses land in the range 1..7 (below MAX_MISSES=8, so gameover at line 130-133/missStar never fires either), the round can neither complete nor fail -- it is permanently unresolvable.
confirmed.evidence: Test 'a round with ANY miss below the gameover threshold' (in test_grand-finale.js): missed 3 stars, collected 17 (collected+misses=20, confirmed), then ran 500 more update(48) calls (24s simulated) plus a real sleep. Got game.state==='playing' and game.round===0 forever, with g.stars.length===0 (no further spawns) -- expected the round to either advance (round->1) or end (gameover), got neither. Contrast: the immediately-following happy-path test, using the identical update()/setTimeout transition mechanism, collects all 60 stars across 3 rounds with zero misses and does reach game.state==='won' cleanly -- proving the transition code itself works and is simply never reached once any miss count lands between 1 and 7.
confirmed.fix:
Minimal fix - line 153, drop game.misses from the spawn gate so misses no longer consume the round's spawn budget:

  if(uncollected<8&&spawnTimer>=si&&(uncollected+game.collected)<PER_ROUND){

The round then always resolves: keep spawning until 20 are collected (advance via line 185), or hit MAX_MISSES=8 (gameover via missStar). Termination is guaranteed. This matches the author's own stated intent in the line-182 comment ("only complete on full collection"), the HUD's "Collected: x/20", and the title screen's "3 rounds of 20 stars each". Verified: a 2-misses-per-round playthrough reaches 'won' with total 60.

EXPLICIT WARNING about the obvious alternative fix: the tempting move is to fill in the empty branch at lines 180-184 to complete the round on collected+misses>=PER_ROUND. That does unlock the game, but it silently redefines a "round of 20" - a player who misses 3 finishes the round at 17/20 while the HUD still reads "17/20", and reaches the win screen having collected fewer than 60 stars, contradicting the title text. Prefer the gate fix.

Second code path to clean up either way: delete the dead 180-184 branch. With the gate fix its outer condition can still fire (e.g. collected 15 + misses 5 = 20) and execute an empty body - harmless, but it is the exact code that concealed this bug. Note the gameover path is correctly ordered and needs no change: missStar sets state='gameover' inside the star loop (line 166), and line 179's `if(game.state==='gameover')return;` runs before the round-complete block, so gameover correctly wins over round completion. roundDone also needs no change: it is reset in buildRound, and resetGame -> buildRound(0) clears it after 'won'.

confirmed.title: MAX_DEATHS_PER_LEVEL is dead code: the per-level death tally resets itself after every single death, so 'gameover' can never be reached by dying
confirmed.symptom: A player who dies over and over on the same level never sees the 'Out of Tries' game-over screen, no matter how badly they play. The on-screen 'Lives X/3' counter also never decreases below the full amount, and the 'Clean streak' counter increments even on a level the player just died on -- both readouts are simply wrong. The game silently gives infinite retries on every level; the entire death-limit/game-over ending is unreachable content.
confirmed.rootCause: die() (index.html line 108-111) does: `game.levelDeaths++; if(game.levelDeaths>=MAX_DEATHS_PER_LEVEL){game.state='gameover';return;} buildLevel(game.level);` -- but buildLevel() (line 49-62) unconditionally executes `game.levelDeaths=0;` (line 55) as part of rebuilding the level for the retry. Since die()'s own non-terminal branch calls buildLevel(game.level) to set up the retry, it immediately erases the counter it just incremented, every single time, before the next death can add to it. levelDeaths can therefore never be observed above 1 mid-flight and is always back to 0 by the time die() returns, so the `>=3` check at line 110 can mathematically never fire from real play.
confirmed.evidence: Standalone script and the test suite both reproduce this identically: resetGame() -> 30 consecutive real obstacle-collision deaths on level 1 (teleporting the live player onto obstacles[0] and calling the real update()/die() path each time). Observed on every single one of the 30 deaths: `game.state === 'playing'` (never 'gameover'), `game.levelDeaths === 0` (never accumulates, never reaches 1 by the time control returns to the caller), `game.deaths` correctly incrementing 1..30 (total-death counter is fine, only the per-level tally is broken). Expected per the MAX_DEATHS_PER_LEVEL=3 constant: gameover should have fired on the 3rd death.

confirmed.title: Completing a haiku never advances the game — advanceIfDone() is permanently dead code
confirmed.symptom: The player drags every syllable tile into its correct slot for haiku 1/8. All three lines flash green with a check-mark, particles burst, the win chime (sndWin2) plays, and the score visibly increases. Then... nothing. The board stays exactly as solved, forever. No haiku 2 ever loads, and the game can never reach the 'won' screen no matter how long the player waits or how many more (already-filled) slots they fiddle with. 7 of the 8 haikus, and the entire win screen, are unreachable through normal play.
confirmed.rootCause: advanceIfDone() (index.html:198-213) is gated by `if(game.correctAnim<=0 && game.pendingAdvance && dragging===null)`. `game.pendingAdvance` is read at line 201 and reset to false at line 202, but it is never assigned `true` anywhere in the file — a full-file grep for `pendingAdvance` and for `[Aa]dvance` turns up only its declaration/read/reset. checkDone() (line 166-196), which correctly detects all 3 lines matching and awards score, never sets it either. Since `game.pendingAdvance` is always falsy, the body of advanceIfDone() — the only code that increments `game.hIdx`, calls `buildHaiku()` for the next haiku, or transitions `game.state` to 'won' — can never execute.
confirmed.evidence: Drove the real loop via window.__cfq.snapToSlot(tile, slotIndex) for every slot of haiku 1, matching each slot's expected word from HAIKUS[0].lines (mirroring what a player's drag-and-drop does). Confirmed placement succeeded: all 3 slots-per-line show `!s.wrong`, and game.score rose from 0 to a positive value (checkDone's own success branch ran). Then pumped update(16) for 3000ms of simulated frame time (far past the 800ms correctAnim window the game's own code uses). Result: `game.hIdx` stayed 0 (expected 1), `game.state` stayed 'playing' with the same haiku still on screen. Repeating this for haiku 2 and 3 in a row produced identical stalls each time. Attempting to clear all 8 haikus in sequence never reached `game.state==='won'` (test 'solving all 8 haikus reaches the won state' failed). This reproduced in 6 direct assertions across the suite (1 single-haiku, 3 in the three-in-a-row loop, 1 win-path, plus the win-restart precondition and the post-hint-advance check as downstream consequences of the same root cause) — 10 total failures, all traceable to this one dead flag.

confirmed.title: hint() never advances past its first suggestion, and following it a second time undoes progress instead of continuing it
confirmed.symptom: The player presses the hint button (or H key), clicks the hexagon it highlights (a real, helpful move that lowers the mismatch count), and then presses hint again hoping for the next step. Hint highlights the exact same hexagon again. If the player follows it a second time -- the natural thing to do when a 'hint' keeps pointing at the same spot -- they undo the move they just made, making the board worse than before they started using hints at all.
confirmed.rootCause: hint() at index.html:149-155 always reads game.solution[game.solution.length-1] and never mutates or advances game.solution (no pop/shift, no cursor). It is a static readout of the LAST move used to scramble the level, not a stateful 'what should I click next' guide, so it cannot track anything the player has already done.
confirmed.evidence: Built level index 2 (solution length 4, no locked hex). hint() -> {c:0,r:2}. Clicked (0,2) via doFlip: mismatch went 21 -> 18 (a real, correct improvement). Called hint() again: still {c:0,r:2} (identical cell). Followed it again: mismatch went 18 -> 21 -- back to worse than the starting point. Reproduced in test_hex-flip.js under '--- hint: does it advance, or does it consume-and-freeze? ---'.

confirmed.title: Rounds 4-5 spawn pieces pre-rotated with no in-game way to rotate them, permanently soft-locking the game
confirmed.symptom: From round 4 onward, most jigsaw pieces render rotated (90/180/270 degrees). Dragging a rotated piece exactly onto its correct target position never snaps it into place -- it just shakes/rejects (miss sound + screen shake) every single time, no matter how many times it's retried. The round, and therefore the whole game, can never be finished; there is no error, no game-over screen, nothing -- the player is just stuck in round 4 forever with only Pause/Mute available.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html line 76: buildRound() assigns each piece a random rot of 0/90/180/270 once ROT_ENABLED (rnd>=4). Line 89: tryPlace()'s rotOk=!ROT_ENABLED||p.rot===0 requires rot===0 to ever place a piece. Grepping the entire 220-line file for any code that ever mutates p.rot after creation (rotate button, keydown handler, double-click/right-click, touch gesture) returns nothing -- only the two draw()-time ctx.rotate() calls and the tryPlace() check reference it. The title screen even advertises this as a feature ('Later rounds add pieces and rotation') but the corresponding player control to rotate a piece was never implemented.
confirmed.evidence: Driving the real onDown/onMove/onUp mouse listeners (not a hook shortcut) through rounds 1->2->3->4 all worked (9, 9, then 12 of 12 pieces placed each round, verified by g.isDone() and game.round incrementing 1->2->3->4). On round 4, buildRound's own seeded RNG (deterministic, seed = round*7621+13, independent of play path) produced 13 of 16 pieces with rot!==0, e.g. {idx:4,rot:180},{idx:13,rot:90},{idx:8,rot:270}. Test: 'a competent player, dragging every piece exactly onto its target, can complete round 4' -> FAIL (isDone() stayed false after 8 full passes). 'clearing round 4 should advance to round 5' -> FAIL (got round 4, expected 5). 'more attempts eventually place the stuck pieces' -> FAIL after 5 additional full passes -- the same 13 pieces (by idx) remained unplaced every time. An independent probe calling buildRound(5) directly showed the same defect there too (14 of 16 pieces rot!==0), so both of the game's final two rounds (40% of its 5-round content) are affected and this is not a one-off seed.
confirmed.fix:
Add the missing rotate control -- the only fix that preserves the advertised feature. Works for mouse AND touch with no new UI by treating a tap (press with no drag) as a rotate:

In onDown, after `dragging=p;`, record the press origin: `dragSX=x; dragSY=y;` (and track the latest pointer as lastX/lastY in onMove).
In onUp, before `game.moves++`:
  if(dragging && ROT_ENABLED && Math.abs(lastX-dragSX)<4 && Math.abs(lastY-dragSY)<4){
    dragging.rot=(dragging.rot+90)%360; sndSnap(); dragging=null; return;  // tap = rotate; not a move, not a miss
  }
tryPlace's existing rotOk=!ROT_ENABLED||p.rot===0 check then becomes satisfiable and needs no change.

ALSO REQUIRED (second broken path): line 129 must drop its `*0` -- change ctx.rotate(-p.rot*Math.PI/180*0) to ctx.rotate(-p.rot*Math.PI/180) -- or every rotated piece's label renders sideways/upside-down. This is a separate dead code path from the same abandoned feature.

WARNING -- the two tempting one-liner fixes are both WRONG because the flag is cleared in one place but not the others: setting ROT_ENABLED=false (line 64) or forcing rotOk=true (line 89) unlocks the round, but line 76 still assigns rot, so pieces keep RENDERING rotated via line 124 while snapping regardless (visually incoherent), and drawHUD (line 138) keeps printing "Rotated pieces · " off the same ROT_ENABLED flag while the title screen (line 149) still advertises rotation. Any such fix must additionally strip the rot assignment at line 76, the ctx.rotate at line 124, the HUD string at 138, and the title-screen line at 149.

confirmed.title: Pausing right after finishing a round permanently stalls it
confirmed.symptom: Player collects the 15th (final) star, HUD reads 15/15, then hits Space (or the pause button) - something a player very plausibly does right after finishing. When they unpause, the round never advances: the HUD stays frozen at 15/15 forever, the round counter never increments, and there is no on-screen indication anything is wrong or what to do.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html, inside update()'s star-collection block (~line 136): on reaching STARS_NEEDED, a `setTimeout(()=>{ if(game.state!=='playing')return; ...game.round=roundNo+1...buildRound(...) },500)` is scheduled. If the player pauses (game.state becomes 'paused') at any point during that 500ms real-time window, the guard silently drops the entire round-advance when the timeout fires, and nothing ever reschedules it.
