confirmed.evidence: Reproduced directly: resetGame() -> collectUntil(15) -> g.game.collected===15, g.game.state==='playing' (both exact matches). Then g.togglePause(); await sleep(700) (letting the 500ms timer fire while paused); g.togglePause() to resume. Result: g.game.round stayed 0 (expected 1), g.game.collected stayed 15, g.stars.length stayed 21 with 6 uncollected - even after 50 further update() calls and another 500ms of real time. The round only unsticks if the player happens to fly over one of the 6 leftover (visually identical) bonus stars, which re-triggers the same threshold check with state now 'playing' and succeeds - but nothing tells the player that's what's needed.

confirmed.title: Pausing right after the final life is lost leaves a 'playing but dead' zombie state
confirmed.symptom: Player loses their last life to a cloud, then pauses within about 400ms. After unpausing, the game keeps running in state='playing' with the HUD showing 0 hearts (all hollow), as if invincible, until the next cloud graze finally ends the game for real.
confirmed.rootCause: Same guard pattern as above, on the death path (~line 171): `setTimeout(()=>{ if(game.state!=='playing')return; game.state='gameover'; },400)`. Pausing during that 400ms window causes the guard to drop the transition to 'gameover' when it fires.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced directly: resetGame() -> lost all 3 lives via repeated cloud collisions -> g.game.lives===0, g.game.state==='playing'. Then g.togglePause(); await sleep(700); g.togglePause(). Result: g.game.state stayed 'playing' (expected 'gameover'), lives remained 0. Confirmed self-healing on a later probe: one more cloud collision correctly re-triggered the death check and reached a real 'gameover' about 400ms later, since that timeout wasn't interrupted.

confirmed.title: Grabbing one extra star before a round transition double-credits the life bonus
confirmed.symptom: If the player, in the ordinary course of flying around, happens to pick up one more (already-visible) star in the roughly 500ms between finishing a round (15/15) and the round actually changing, they receive the round-completion life bonus (lives*5 points) twice instead of once - free, unearned points, no pause or unusual input required.
confirmed.rootCause: Each round spawns STARS_NEEDED+6 = 21 stars against a 15-star requirement (index.html ~line 68), so 6 'bonus' stars remain uncollected right when the quota is met. The collection loop re-checks `if(game.collected>=STARS_NEEDED)` on every single star pickup, not just the one that first crossed the threshold, so a second pickup during the transition window schedules a second round-advance setTimeout carrying the same captured `roundNo`. When both timeouts fire, both pass the `state==='playing'` guard (the round has already been rebuilt as 'playing' by the first one) and each independently adds `lifeBonus=game.lives*5` to the score.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced directly: resetGame() -> collectUntil(15) -> scoreAtQuota=360 (game.score at exactly 15/15, lives still 3). Immediately (before the 500ms elapsed) flew onto one more available star, worth 30 points (scoreAfterOvershoot-scoreBefore=30). After 700ms real wait, final score was 420. Expected final score with a single life-bonus credit: 360+30+(3*5)=405. Actual 420 = 405+15, i.e. the 15-point life bonus was applied a second time.
confirmed.fix:
One operator, index.html line 133: change `if(game.collected>=STARS_NEEDED){` to `if(game.collected===STARS_NEEDED){`. game.collected only ever increments by 1 from 0 (reset in buildRound, line 83), so `===` is crossed exactly once per round and the round-advance timeout can only be scheduled once. Verified on a patched copy: single credit even with 3 overshoot pickups; round still advances, collected resets, game stays playable.

Explicitly: this fix leaves NO second broken code path. There is no flag to clear in two places, and both setTimeout guards (round-advance +500ms at line 137, gameover +400ms at line 172) are unaffected. A flag-based alternative would actually be worse here — it would need clearing in buildRound AND on the 'won' path (which never calls buildRound), which is exactly the two-place-clear hazard this fix avoids. Note the existing state!=='playing' guard should be kept; it still does its original job of preventing the round-advance from resurrecting a gameover.

confirmed.title: Double-clicking/tapping UNLOCK while the unlock animation is still playing skips an entire round
confirmed.symptom: A player who double-clicks or double-taps the UNLOCK button (very plausible on a touch-friendly 120x40 canvas button with zero cooldown) sees the round counter jump by two instead of one - the next round's puzzle is never shown or required, and score/streak are awarded twice for one solve.
confirmed.rootCause: tryUnlock() (index.html:97-120) lacks the same kind of guard useHint() has (index.html:89, `if(game.state!=='playing'||...)return;`). Its setTimeout callback (index.html:111-116) closes over live `game.round` rather than the round number captured at click time, so a second stacked callback reads the already-incremented round and advances again.
confirmed.evidence: Test 'probe: double-unlock while advance is pending': started at round 5, called g.tryUnlock() twice with tumblers still matching target both times (confirmed via allMatch()), awaited 900ms for both scheduled timeouts, and observed game.round go to 7 (not 6) - expected/got both logged in the suite as 'expected round 6, observed round 7'.

confirmed.title: Same double-unlock race can end the game one round early (round 10 is never presented)
confirmed.symptom: A player who double-clicks UNLOCK on the second-to-last round is dropped straight onto the 'Unlocked!' win screen having only solved 9 of the advertised 10 rounds; round 10's puzzle, and the chance to earn its score, is silently skipped.
confirmed.rootCause: Same missing re-entrancy guard on tryUnlock() as above, combined with the win-check reading `game.round` live inside the setTimeout at index.html:112 instead of a value captured when the click was handled.
confirmed.evidence: Test 'probe: double-unlock on the second-to-last round can end the game early': started at round 9 (buildRound(TOTAL-1)), solved it, called tryUnlock() twice synchronously, awaited 900ms, and observed game.state become 'won' - logged as 'game reached "won" after only solving 9 of 10 rounds'.

confirmed.title: Re-entering the exit tile before the transition timeout fires double-scores and skips a level
confirmed.symptom: A player who overshoots the exit and steps back onto it (a natural correction in a game whose whole premise is mirrored, easy-to-fumble controls) before the 500ms win animation finishes gets awarded a second score bonus for the same arrival, and the run silently skips the next maze entirely -- the level counter jumps by 2 instead of 1.
confirmed.rootCause: tryMove() (index.html:118-161) has no 'transitioning' guard: game.state stays 'playing' for the full 500ms between touching the exit and the scheduled setTimeout that does game.level++ and either buildMaze(game.level) or sets state='won' (index.html:142-160). Nothing stops the win-check block from re-firing if the player steps off the exit cell and back onto it before that timeout resolves, which stacks a second independent setTimeout on top of the first.
confirmed.evidence: Full 8-level playthrough baseline: reaching level 0's exit gave score=260, then after the normal 700ms wait state advanced cleanly to level=1 (score stayed 260 until the timeout, then level became 1). Re-entry probe: reached level 0 exit at score=260, then executed one legal step off the exit + one legal step back onto it (both valid tryMove calls, no walls crossed) *before* awaiting the transition -- score immediately jumped to 458 (got 458, expected 260, still synchronous). After waiting 1200ms for all queued timeouts, game.level went from 0 to 2 (got 2, expected 1) -- level 1 was never played. A second probe driven through 6 real level-clears to reach level 6 legitimately, then hammered the exit 3 extra times, produced level jumping all the way to 10 (past TOTAL=8) though the game still safely resolved to state='won' rather than crashing.

confirmed.title: Title screen's own instructions promise a control path that does not exist
confirmed.symptom: The title panel (panelDraw('Mirror Nav', [...])) tells the player: 'Click / tap or press any key to begin.' A player who tries the advertised keyboard route (arrow keys, Enter, Space, any letter) sees nothing happen and may conclude the game is frozen or broken, especially before they've found the mouse/touch alternative.
confirmed.rootCause: The keydown handler (index.html:193-203) opens with pause/mute handling for 'p'/'P'/'Escape'/'m'/'M', then unconditionally returns for every other key whenever game.state !== 'playing' (index.html:198: `if(game.state!=='playing')return;`). No key ever transitions the game out of the title screen -- only the mousedown handler (index.html:393-398) and the touchend tap-detection branch (index.html:213-217) call resetGame() when state !== 'playing'.
confirmed.evidence: Dispatched real KeyboardEvent('keydown') objects for 'ArrowRight', ' ' (space), 'Enter', and 'a' on window.document while game.state was 'title': state remained 'title' after all four (verified via g.game.state). A subsequent real MouseEvent('mousedown') dispatched on the canvas element did transition state to 'playing' immediately, confirming the mouse/touch path works and only the keyboard path described in the game's own text is the one that's missing.

confirmed.title: SUBMIT has no lock during its own post-success transition, so rapid/repeated submits skip rounds and inflate score
confirmed.symptom: A player who double-clicks or double-taps SUBMIT right after getting it right (or just clicks it again quickly out of impatience/excitement) silently skips one or more rounds outright - they see round 1's target, submit it correctly once, and the game jumps straight past round 2 (or further) without ever showing/building it, while their score is inflated by an extra scoring pass over content they never actually attempted.
confirmed.rootCause: submit() (index.html:132-175) is guarded only by `if(game.playing||game.paused||game.state!=='playing')return;` - none of those flags are set during the 700ms `setTimeout` that performs the actual `game.round++;buildRound(game.round)` (line 150-159). Because that callback reads `game.round` live (not a captured snapshot) and the grid/target haven't changed yet, a second submit() call inside the 700ms window re-scores the identical unchanged grid and schedules a second callback; when the first callback's buildRound() bumps game.round, the second callback (firing shortly after) reads the now-incremented value and advances again, compounding once per extra submit.
confirmed.evidence: Test-suite probe (test_music-compose.js, 'double-submit during the post-success transition window'): after resetGame() + one perfect grid + two submit() calls 50ms apart, game.round settled at 3 instead of the expected 2 - one full round was skipped by a single extra click. A follow-up standalone probe (8 submit() calls at 20ms intervals, well within human multi-click speed) took the game from round 1 (score 0) straight through to the real 'won' screen (round 8 fully cleared, score 168) in under 200ms of input, without ever building or displaying rounds 2 through 8. draw() afterward still ran without throwing and game.target/game.grid remained valid 6-row arrays, so this is a progression-integrity bug (content skipped, score inflated), not a crash or freeze.

confirmed.title: Winning permanently instant-re-wins the game on every future restart
confirmed.symptom: After a player reaches the win screen once, clicking to play again shows the same win screen after only one click, no matter what they do; upgrades, bubbles, and prestige stop mattering because the game re-declares victory instantly on every future session.
confirmed.rootCause: resetGame() (line 173) zeroes game.total in memory for a fresh round, but its last statement unconditionally calls loadGame() (line 181), which restores game.total from the localStorage save (line 219: game.total=Math.max(0,Number(data.total)||0)). That save was written by maybeWin()'s saveGame() call (line 169) at the moment of winning, so it already holds total>=WIN_TARGET. Since game.total is only ever incremented elsewhere in the code and there is no in-game 'erase save' control, every subsequent resetGame() (called from pointerDown on both the 'won' and 'title' screens) restores a total that is already past the threshold, and the first silenceBubble() -> maybeWin() call (line 164) re-triggers state='won' immediately.
confirmed.evidence: Test forced a real win (total crossed WIN_TARGET=250000 via an actual silenceBubble call, save confirmed written to localStorage), then called g.resetGame() -- the exact function pointerDown() calls on the won screen -- and read state immediately: expected hours=0/total<250000, got game.hours=1 and game.total=250000.5. One further real click (g.silenceBubble) then produced game.state='won' again with total=250001.5, i.e. the fresh session re-won itself off a single click.

confirmed.title: Stray click during the post-success celebration window is scored as a wrong answer (false game-over in endless mode)
confirmed.symptom: Right after correctly finishing a pattern, tapping the grid again (very plausible: the win animation/particles are still showing) registers as a mistake -- costing a life outside endless mode, or ending the run outright in endless mode -- even though the player did nothing wrong.
confirmed.rootCause: In clickCell's success branch (index.html ~line 86-113), round++/buildRound() are deferred to a real setTimeout(...,600). Nothing in that branch changes game.phase away from 'input' or clears playerInput synchronously, so the click guard `state==='playing' && phase==='input'` (line 63) still lets clicks through for ~600ms. Any extra click compares idx against sequence[playerInput.length-1], which is now undefined (index past the just-completed sequence) -- always a mismatch -- so it falls into the wrong-answer branch (mistakes++, streak=0, lives-- or, in endless, immediate gameover).
confirmed.evidence: Test: solved round 0 cleanly (lives=3, streak=1), then called clickCell(0) with no delay. Observed lives 3 -> 2, mistakes +1, streak reset to 0 (expected: no penalty, celebration in progress). Separately, after clearing all 10 rounds into endless mode and solving one more endless round, an identical stray clickCell(0) drove game.state to 'gameover' (expected: still playing, endless run continuing) -- confirmed via test_pattern-copy.js 'bug candidate' section, all assertions passed proving the buggy behavior occurs.
confirmed.fix:
Close the input window synchronously in BOTH branches by introducing an inert phase. update() only acts on phase 'transition' and 'show', so any other value makes update() a no-op while the pending setTimeout owns the transition — no new state machine needed.

1) Success branch — insert immediately before `setTimeout(()=>{game.round++;...},600);` (line 107):
     game.phase='celebrate';litCell=-1;

2) Miss branch — insert immediately after `playerInput=[];` (line 71), i.e. BEFORE the `if(!game.endless)` check, so it covers all three exits (life lost + retry, lives<=0 gameover, endless gameover):
     game.phase='celebrate';litCell=-1;

Do not reuse 'transition' here: update() would advance transitionT past 280ms and flip to 'show', re-flashing the OLD sequence for the remainder of the window before buildRound() replaces it. 'celebrate' (any name update() doesn't match) avoids that.

Both existing setTimeouts already set game.phase='transition' when they fire, so recovery is unchanged. Side effects are benign: drawGrid's `playerHit` highlight (requires phase==='input') stops during the window, and drawHUD falls to its else branch showing "Repeat it! (n / n)" exactly as it does today.

Optional hardening (not required for this bug): the 450ms gameover timeouts leave state==='playing'; the phase fix above already blocks clicks there, so no separate guard is needed.

confirmed.title: The 'won' end screen ("All Remembered! ✓") is dead code -- game.state is never actually set to 'won'
confirmed.symptom: A player who clears all 10 rounds is never shown the designed acknowledgment screen for finishing the fixed campaign; the game silently rolls into endless mode with no transition screen at all.
confirmed.rootCause: draw() (index.html line 230) renders a full panel for game.state==='won', and onDown() (line 258) treats 'won' as a restart-eligible state, but no code path anywhere assigns game.state='won' -- confirmed by grep across the whole file. The only post-round-10 transition (lines 108-112) sets game.endless=true and calls buildRound(), never touching state.
confirmed.evidence: Grepped the full source for `state='won'` / `state="won"` -- zero assignments found, only the two reads. Drove the game through all 10 rounds via update()+clickCell() and checked game.state after every one of the 10 round-completions: never equal to 'won' (flag stayed false through the whole loop).

confirmed.title: game.level never advances after winning a level -- the player is permanently stuck on level 2's puzzle
confirmed.symptom: The HUD always reads 'Level 1' no matter how many puzzles are solved. Pressing Enter or clicking the drawn 'Next Level' button on the won screen returns to play, but after winning once, twice, or three times in a row, the player keeps being served the IDENTICAL level-2 puzzle (same grid, same pipe layout, byte-for-byte) forever. Level 3 and beyond, and the level-20 'Master' tier badge, are unreachable through any normal input.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:228-229 -- game.level is set ONLY inside resetGame() (to 1) and continueGame() (to a saved value). buildLevel(lv) (line 105) uses its lv argument to size the grid/pick pipe types/set parMoves, but never assigns it to game.level. Both real advance paths call buildLevel(game.level+1) without ever updating game.level: the Enter handler at line 408 (`if(game.state==='won')buildLevel(game.level+1);`) and pointerDown's won-branch at lines 392-394, which additionally contains a dead tautology `game.level=Math.min(game.level+1,9999)===game.level?game.level:game.level;` -- comparing the incremented value to the current value is never true, so the ':' branch (assign game.level to itself) always executes, making that line a no-op. Since game.level is stuck at 1, every subsequent win computes buildLevel(1+1)=buildLevel(2) again, replaying the same seeded puzzle (rng=seeded(lv*5003+11+...), lv always 2) forever.
confirmed.evidence: Test log: after resetGame()+solve+win+Enter, game.level stayed 1 (expected 2). A second full solve+win+Enter cycle produced a grid fingerprint (GCOLS/GROWS/all cell masks) byte-identical to the first advance's grid (fp2 === fp3 was true; expected false). After three full win cycles, localStorage['pipeflow_save_v1'].lastLevel was 2 (expected 4), and bestMoves only ever contained the key "1" (expected keys "1","2","3"). The mouse path (dispatching a real mousedown at the 'Next Level' button's coordinates, computed from panelDraw()'s own bw=220,bh=48,bx=W/2-bw/2,by=py+ph-84 formula) reproduced the identical defect independently of the keyboard path.

confirmed.title: Consequence: save/continue silently discards all real progress
confirmed.symptom: Closing and reopening the game (or just clicking Continue) always resumes at level 2, even after a session in which the player actually solved three consecutively-harder puzzles.
confirmed.rootCause: continueGame() (index.html:229) reads save.lastLevel, which solveFlow()'s win branch (line 181) writes as game.level+1. Since game.level never leaves 1 (see above), lastLevel is always persisted as 2 regardless of actual play history.
confirmed.evidence: Test: after a 3-cycle win session in one JSDOM window, a second fresh JSDOM instance was preloaded with that session's real pipeflow_save_v1 value and called continueGame(); it resumed at level 2. The separate savedAfter3.lastLevel === 2 check (after three real wins, where a correctly-progressing game would have saved 4) directly shows the saved value is fixed at 2 independent of how many levels were actually solved.
confirmed.fix:
Make buildLevel() own the level number — one line, at index.html line 160, changing:

  game.moves=0;game.state='playing';game.streak=0;flowCells=new Set();undoStack=[];

to:

  game.level=lv;game.moves=0;game.state='playing';game.streak=0;flowCells=new Set();undoStack=[];

This is consistent with all four existing callers: resetGame() already sets game.level=1 before buildLevel(1), continueGame() already sets game.level=lv before buildLevel(lv) (both become harmless redundant assignments), and the two win-advance paths pass game.level+1, which now actually takes effect. It simultaneously repairs the HUD level readout, the per-level bestMoves/bestTime keys (currently all collapsing onto key "1"), the gridForLevel/pipeTypesForLevel difficulty ramp, and the lastLevel persistence.

EXPLICIT WARNING — the obvious fix leaves a second path broken: the tempting patch is to repair only the self-referential tautology at line 393 in the pointerDown won-branch (e.g. rewriting it to `game.level=Math.min(game.level+1,9999);`). That fixes ONLY mouse/touch advancement. The Enter keydown won-branch at line 408 (`if(game.state==='won')buildLevel(game.level+1);`) has no level assignment at all, so a keyboard player would still never progress and would still save lastLevel=2 forever. Both call sites funnel through buildLevel, so assigning inside buildLevel covers both; if instead patching at the call sites, line 408 must be fixed too.

Optional follow-up (not required for correctness): the title screen reads the page-load `save` snapshot (line 364) rather than loadSave(), so the "Continue from level N" text is stale within a session. Cosmetic only.

confirmed.title: Every one of the 8 puzzles is geometrically unsolvable — the game cannot be won
confirmed.symptom: A player rotates every mirror through its full range in any of the 8 puzzles and the win screen ('Spectrum Aligned') never appears, because no click sequence exists that satisfies the win condition.
confirmed.rootCause: buildPuzzle() (index.html ~lines 51-65) places each mirror at a random (mx,my) independent of the fixed beam angle its colour takes from the prism (checkSolve(), line 98: spreadAng=-0.3+(i/(numC-1||1))*0.6, cast from PRISM_X=180,PRISM_Y=270). A mirror's 40px segment (20px half-length, segHit()) can only ever intersect a line passing within 20px of the mirror's centre, for ANY rotation. Nothing in generation enforces that. checkSolve() requires ALL targets hit simultaneously (line 99), so one unreachable colour per round makes the whole round unsolvable.
confirmed.evidence: Perpendicular distance from mirror centre to its fixed incoming beam, computed from the running game's real mirror/target/prism coordinates (need <=20px to ever be reachable by any rotation): Round 0 'Freedom' mirror = 53.5px (only 'Joy' at 1.1px reachable, so round still unsolvable). Round 1 'Freedom' = 278.8px. Round 2 'Freedom'=27.0px, 'Calm'=195.0px. Round 3: all three targets 121-184px (0/3 reachable). Round 4: 0/4 reachable (93.8-221.3px). Round 5: only 'Calm' reachable at 0.7px, others 33.9-198.7px (0/4 fully). Round 6: 0/5 reachable (43.5-248.6px). Round 7: 0/5 reachable (43.0-207.5px). Confirmed by exhaustive brute force of all 16 discrete click-states per mirror via the game's own checkSolve()/rotateMirror(), and independently by a continuous 0.002-0.005 rad sweep of the full circle (>300-1200 samples per mirror) which never found a hit for any of the flagged targets, in every one of the 8 rounds tested (8/8).

confirmed.title: A round completed at the buzzer is overwritten with a game-over screen by the next frame tick
confirmed.symptom: A player who drops the last correct ingredient into the pot with only a frame or two left on the round clock sees their completed recipe get discarded: instead of advancing to the next round (or, on round 8, to the win screen), the game immediately shows 'Round Failed' even though every ingredient was correctly added and the point was already scored.
confirmed.rootCause: completeRound() (index.html lines 158-191) awards score and schedules a 700ms setTimeout to advance state, but does not stop or bypass the round timer. update() (lines 192-212) keeps ticking game.roundTime every frame regardless, and its own check at lines 207-210 ('if(game.roundLimit>0 && game.roundTime>=game.roundLimit) finishFail();') fires finishFail() (lines 153-157, sets state='gameover' immediately) on the very next update() call if the completion happened close enough to the deadline. Because finishFail() runs synchronously while completeRound()'s transition is still pending, the game-over write wins the race and the round's own scheduled setTimeout later finds state!=='playing' and silently no-ops (line 176: 'if(game.state!=='playing')return;').
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced by ticking update(16) in a loop until game.roundLimit-game.roundTime <= 16 (34000ms round limit, driven down to 16ms remaining using nothing but ordinary per-frame update() calls), then calling tryAdd() on all 4 correct ingredients for the current recipe: state stayed 'playing' and score became 1 (recipe genuinely completed, added.length===4/4). One further g.update(16) call -- the very next animation frame, exactly as real gameplay would produce -- flipped game.state from 'playing' straight to 'gameover' with no intervening 'won'/round-advance. Full console trace: "drove the clock down to 16ms remaining, then completed the recipe; state right after completion was 'playing', score 1; state after one more frame tick: 'gameover'".

confirmed.title: Round-completion transition has no re-entry guard: every real frame during its own 400ms delay re-fires it, so the round counter skips ahead and the score is duplicated
confirmed.symptom: A player who finishes a round sees the round counter and score jump forward by several rounds/multiples of the round bonus almost instantly, with zero extra input, instead of advancing one round at a time. The stated '10 rounds' progression is not reliably experienced - the player can be catapulted from round 1 into round 5-9 (or straight to the 'Race Complete' screen) after playing only the first round's worth of obstacles.
confirmed.rootCause: In update(dt) (index.html, the `if(game.dist>=DIST_PER_ROUND){...}` block inside update()), the round-complete branch adds +50/+75 score and schedules `setTimeout(()=>{game.round++; ...; buildRound(game.round)}, 400)` but never sets a guard flag (unlike the game's own inputLock/paused checks elsewhere). game.state stays 'playing' and game.dist stays >= DIST_PER_ROUND for the full 400ms until the callback fires and buildRound() finally resets dist to 0. Every requestAnimationFrame-driven update() call in that ~400ms window (about 24 real frames at 60fps) re-enters the same block: it re-awards the round bonus and queues yet another independent setTimeout. When the queue drains, each callback independently increments game.round and calls buildRound(game.round), so the round number cascades upward by however many redundant frames elapsed, silently skipping the intervening rounds without the player ever seeing their obstacles.
confirmed.evidence: Baseline (only ONE update() call crosses the line, then update() is never called again before the timeout drains): round advances 0 -> 1, score +50, dist resets to 0 - this is the correct/intended behavior and is exactly what my baseline assertion in test_relay-run.js confirms passes. Realistic test (update() driven at real ~16ms cadence across the same finish line for ~500ms, matching actual rAF timing): run 1 - 19 real frames stepped, round went 0 -> 6, score 0 -> 300 (expected round 0->1, score +50..75); run 2 (fresh process) - round went 0 -> 5, score 0 -> 250. Both runs fail the assertions `roundAfterSustained === roundBeforeSustained + 1` and `scoreAfterSustained - scoreBeforeSustained <= 100` in test_relay-run.js's 'round transition under realistic sustained frame-stepping' section.

confirmed.title: A single calm ripple can fully deplete a boss wave's 3-hit counter without a second click
confirmed.symptom: A player fires one calming ripple at an incoming boss wave (the ones the HUD labels 'hits: 3/3'). The boss dissolves immediately, in the same fraction of a second, instead of surviving to require two more separately-timed ripples the on-screen counter implies.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html lines 152-159: `for(const cr of calmRipples){ if(cr.alpha<=0)continue; for(const pw of pressureWaves){ if(pw.dissolved)continue; if(Math.hypot(pw.wpx-cr.x,pw.wpy-cr.y)<cr.r+10){ pw.hits--; ... if(pw.hits<=0){pw.dissolved=true; ...} } } }` runs every update() frame for the full lifetime of a ripple (up to its ~1.36s alpha decay), and nothing marks a (ripple, wave) pair as already resolved once it registers a hit, so continuous overlap across frames is read as repeated hits.
confirmed.evidence: Constructed a boss wave with the identical shape spawnPressure() produces (hits:3, maxHits:3, boss:true) and drove it through the real update()/collision code via g.emitCalm() + g.update(16). One accepted emitCalm() call produced this exact hits sequence over frames 0-2: hits=2 (frame 0), hits=1 (frame 1), hits=0/dissolved=true (frame 2) with zero additional emitCalm() calls in between. Repeated the identical probe script twice; both runs produced the identical frame-by-frame sequence. A second variant with the click offset by (+50,+30)px from the wave's position instead of dead-on still fully depleted the boss from one click, just later (hits dropped at frames 12, 13, 14 once the growing ripple radius first reached the wave).

confirmed.title: Latent race: a gameover reached during the post-harvest transition window is silently overwritten by the scheduled round-advance
confirmed.symptom: If the choked-threshold gameover is ever reached in the 500ms window right after a round's final harvest (state='transition'), the pending setTimeout that advances the round fires anyway and resurrects the game into 'playing' (or 'won'), erasing the loss with no player-visible error. Currently unreachable in practice only because of the finding above -- if the choke threshold, weed-spawn rate, or round length are ever retuned to make MAX_CHOKED reachable, this becomes a real, silent bug.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html ~lines 107-116: the harvest branch of clickPlot() does `game.state='transition'; setTimeout(()=>{ game.roundStats.push(...); game.round++; if(game.round>=TOTAL){game.state='won';} else {buildRound(game.round);} }, 500);` -- the callback never checks game.state before overwriting it, even though update() (lines 170-173) can independently set game.state='gameover' during that same 'transition' window (its fail-condition check runs for state 'playing'||'transition').
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced by reaching harvested=HARVEST_TARGET-1 via legitimate play, planting fresh unwatered plots, triggering the final harvest (state correctly becomes 'transition'), then choking those growing plots to cross MAX_CHOKED: game.state immediately becomes 'gameover' (choked=6/6) as expected. After waiting out the real 500ms, game.state is 'playing' again (buildRound had silently run), i.e. the gameover was overwritten.

confirmed.title: Rapid double-press of CHECK MATCH during the 600ms advance window skips a round and double-awards score
confirmed.symptom: A player who double-clicks (mouse) or double-taps (touch, which this game explicitly supports via touchstart/touchend) the CHECK MATCH button right after a successful match sees the round counter jump by 2 instead of 1 — one round's shape/shadow puzzle is silently skipped and never shown — while the score is credited twice for what looks like a single match.
confirmed.rootCause: checkMatch() (games/shadow-match/index.html, function at line 101, success branch at lines 108-135) has no transition-lock guard. On a successful match it scores points and schedules `setTimeout(()=>{game.round++; ...; nextRound();},600)` but leaves game.state==='playing' for that whole 600ms. onDown() (line 310) has no guard against re-entering checkMatch() during this window either — it only blocks input when state is 'title'/'won' or game.paused. If checkMatch() fires again before the first timeout runs, shapePos and targetOff are still the same (nextRound() hasn't executed yet), so the second call also succeeds, scores again, and schedules a second `round++; nextRound()`. Both timeouts fire ~600ms later back-to-back, incrementing round twice total for one round played.
confirmed.evidence: Direct-hook reproduction (test_shadow-match.js, 'rapid double-press' block): starting at round 0, one landOnTarget() followed immediately by a second g.checkMatch() (same tick, unmoved position) produced round 2 after the 750ms settle, i.e. +2 instead of +1. Confirmed independently through the REAL input path (not the test hook): using JSDOM MouseEvents dispatched at the actual CHECK MATCH hit-box coordinates (canvas x=480,y=498, satisfying the button's `p.y>H-60 && p.y<H-24 && |p.x-W/2|<70` test) after a real mousedown/mousemove/mouseup drag onto the target — two rapid button presses produced `score right after two button presses: 148` (two payouts) and `round after 750ms: 2` (expected 1). Both reproductions used the game's own onDown/checkMatch code, not a hook shortcut.

confirmed.title: Arrow keys (←/→ or a/d) do not start or continue the game at the title or win screens, contradicting the game's own on-screen instructions
confirmed.symptom: A player who reads the title panel ("Click / tap or press ←/→ to begin.") or the win panel ("Tap for a Focus Run (endless) or press ←/→.") and tries the documented arrow-key control gets no response at all — no error, no visual feedback, the screen just sits there. Mouse click / touch tap is the only input that actually works at these two screens.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html line 83, the keydown listener: `if(e.key==='Escape'...){...} if(game.state!=='playing')return; if(e.key==='ArrowLeft'||e.key==='a')steer(-1); if(e.key==='ArrowRight'||e.key==='d')steer(1);`. The blanket `if(game.state!=='playing')return;` fires before the arrow-key branches are ever reached, so ArrowLeft/ArrowRight/a/d are wired ONLY to steer() during play — there is no keydown handling at all for 'title' or 'won' states, even though the canvas text explicitly tells the player to use them there.
confirmed.evidence: Test: set g.game.state='title' (the real state the page boots into), dispatched a genuine `new KeyboardEvent('keydown',{key:'ArrowRight'})` on document — got state still 'title', expected 'playing'. Repeated with 'ArrowLeft' — same result. Reached 'won' via a full legitimate 20/20 playthrough, dispatched ArrowRight again — got game.endless still false / state still 'won', expected the Focus Run to start. In all three cases the identical mouse path (dispatching a real `mousedown` on the canvas) worked correctly, confirming the break is specific to the keyboard path.

confirmed.title: Dying via an in-band gate collision leaves the fatal gate object un-cleared, so it lingers (dimmed) behind the gameover overlay
confirmed.symptom: On the death screen ("Caught by Obligation"), the red/obligation gate box that just killed the player is still drawn on screen underneath the semi-transparent overlay panel, instead of disappearing the way every other gate does the instant it resolves. Purely visual — the game is still fully playable and a restart clears it.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html line 73 vs 74, inside update()'s in-band collision branch: `}else{game.lives--;...; if(game.lives<=0){finalizeRun();game.state='gameover';return;}}` returns BEFORE the `gate=null;` on the very next line, which every non-fatal miss (and every successful clear, and the theoretical fall-past-bottom branch on line 77) does reach. So the one and only path that actually kills the player in real play (an in-band miss with lives already at 1) is also the one path that skips nulling `gate`. Since draw() calls drawGate() whenever state!=='title' — including 'gameover' — before the panel overlay, the stale gate renders behind the death screen.
confirmed.evidence: Reproduced directly (isolated repro script, not the full suite): g.resetGame(); g.game.lives=1; steered into the red/obligation lane; drove update(16) to resolution. Result: game.state === 'gameover', game.lives === 0, and g.gate === {"y":392.16,"vy":180,"w":160,"left":{"label":"Rest","green":true},"right":{"label":"Settle","green":false}} instead of null. y=392 sits inside the runner's own collision band (390–480), i.e. it's the exact gate that caused the death, still fully populated.
confirmed.fix: index.html line 73: change `if(game.lives<=0){finalizeRun();game.state='gameover';return;}` to `if(game.lives<=0){gate=null;finalizeRun();game.state='gameover';return;}`. No second path is left broken — I checked the other early-returns explicitly: the 'won' return on line 75 already executes after line 74's gate=null, and line 77's branch already nulls gate before its own lives check. Do NOT instead fix this in draw() by excluding 'gameover' from the `state!=='title'` guard on line 141 — that same block also draws the runner, particles and popups, which are intentionally visible on the death screen.

confirmed.title: Bubble spawn direction is deterministic per round, and in round 2 (every single playthrough) all 5 bubbles fly away from the archer and can never be hit
confirmed.symptom: The player sees a stigma bubble appear at the right edge and drift only farther right until it vanishes; no shot, however well-timed or aimed, can ever pop it. In round 2 specifically, this happens to every one of that round's 5 bubbles, on every playthrough -- the round is only ever 'won' by burning through all 8 shots into empty air and letting the built-in shot-exhaustion safety net force them closed.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:43 spawnBubbles() sets each bubble's horizontal speed as `spd:Math.max(30,...)*(rng()>0.5?1:-1)`. index.html:57 then does `b.x-=b.spd*(dt/1000)`, so a bubble with negative spd has x INCREASE every frame (moves away from the archer at x=80) instead of decreasing. Bubbles spawn at `x=W+r+i*140` (index.html:43), i.e. always beyond the canvas width (960), and the arrow's own live boundary is `arrow.x>W+10` (index.html:61) -- so a bubble born with negative spd starts already past the point any arrow can ever reach and only moves farther away; it can structurally never re-enter play. Because spawnBubbles seeds its RNG purely from the round number (`rng=seeded(rnd*8123+5)`, index.html:43) with no player-input or time entropy, this isn't occasional bad luck -- it is the exact same outcome every time that round is played.
confirmed.evidence: Calling window.__cfq.startRound(2) twice independently produced the identical spd array both times: [-69,-111,-105,-94,-111] -- all 5 negative, all 5 unreachable. For comparison: round 1 = [67,80,110,-100,-78] (2/5 unreachable), round 4 = [149,-104,-161,148,-111] (3/5), round 5 = [-153,-140,-150,136,-151] (4/5), round 10 = [151,-257,154,257,177] (1/5). The game does not soft-lock -- the round still completes via the MAX_SHOTS_PER_ROUND(8) exhaustion safety net that force-closes remaining bubbles -- but the stated core action ('burst the stigma bubbles') is guaranteed to be a no-op for an entire round, every time, regardless of player skill.
confirmed.fix:
Delete the random sign multiplier at index.html:43 so every bubble drifts left toward the archer:

  const spd=Math.max(30,Math.floor(d.spdMin+rng()*(d.spdMax-d.spdMin)));

(i.e. drop the trailing `*(rng()>0.5?1:-1)`).

NO SECOND CODE PATH IS LEFT BROKEN: I grepped `spd` across the file -- it is written only at line 43 and read only at line 57. With all speeds positive, the round-completion check at line 64 (`b.x<-b.r`) resolves naturally and the line 62 shot-exhaustion backstop reverts to a true backstop. One deliberate, benign side effect: removing that rng() call shifts the seeded stream, so every round gets a new (still valid) layout. If preserving the existing layouts is required, the stream-neutral alternative is to change line 57 to `b.x-=Math.abs(b.spd)*(dt/1000)` -- but that leaves a now-meaningless sign stored on the bubble, so the line 43 fix is preferred.

confirmed.title: clearLines() drops the wrong rows when 2+ lines complete simultaneously, leaving full rows physically stuck on the board
confirmed.symptom: A player clears two, three, or (the flagship) four lines at once with a Tetris drop. The score increases, the line counter and Back-to-back/All-Clear bonuses fire correctly, but one or more of the rows that were supposedly just cleared are still sitting on the board, completely solid, exactly as before. The stack never actually shrinks by the full amount the player just earned credit for.
confirmed.rootCause: In clearLines() (index.html, inside the `if(cleared){...}` block, the row-removal loop: `for(const r of rowsToFlash){grid.splice(r,1);grid.unshift(Array(COLS).fill(null));}`). rowsToFlash is computed once, in descending order, against the pre-clear board. But each `grid.splice(r,1)` followed by `grid.unshift(...)` shifts every remaining row's index by one (because unshift inserts at index 0, not at the position that was just removed). The loop keeps using the original stale indices for subsequent full rows, so after the first removal the second 'r' no longer points at a full row -- it can point at an already-empty row instead, while a genuinely full row is skipped and survives.
confirmed.evidence: Direct isolation via the real, unmodified clearLines() (fillRows helper + calling g.clearLines()): 1 row filled -> lines 0->1, 0 rows left full (correct). 2 rows filled -> lines 0->2, but 1 row still fully occupied afterward. 3 rows filled -> lines 0->3, 1 row still full. 4 rows filled (a Tetris) -> lines 0->4, 2 rows still full. Reproduced with a genuine hard-drop too (not just direct grid setup): stacked 8 columns two rows deep, then retried resetGame() until the randomly-dealt piece was the O-piece ('Joy', 2x2), moved it into the last two open columns, and called the real g.hardDrop() -> lock() -> clearLines() path. Result: game.lines went 0->2 (score credited for a 2-line clear, 336 points) but the grid dump afterward showed row 19 still completely solid ('##########'), i.e. one of the two 'cleared' rows never actually left the board.
confirmed.fix:
In clearLines() (index.html:123), iterate the collected row indices in ASCENDING order instead of the descending order they were gathered in. For rows below r, splice's -1 shift and unshift's +1 shift cancel, so lower indices stay valid; rows above r are not yet visited.

Replace:
  for(const r of rowsToFlash){grid.splice(r,1);grid.unshift(Array(COLS).fill(null));}
With:
  for(const r of [...rowsToFlash].sort((a,b)=>a-b)){grid.splice(r,1);grid.unshift(Array(COLS).fill(null));}

Copy before sorting is deliberate: game.flashRows=rowsToFlash (line 120) must keep the original pre-clear indices for the flash overlay, and sorting in place would not corrupt them numerically but the copy keeps intent clear and avoids coupling.

Verified against a patched copy of the real file (_adv_tetris_fixcheck.js): n=1/2/3/4 all leave 0 cells and 0 rows full, and a non-adjacent case (rows 17 and 19 full with a single marker block at row 18 col 3) correctly clears 2 lines and settles the marker to r19c3.

No second code path is left broken: clearLines() is the only place rows are removed and it is only called from lock(). One intended side effect to expect: the isAllClear check at line 137 runs after removal, so it is currently suppressed by this bug; after the fix the All-Clear +1000*level bonus will begin firing correctly. That is the designed behaviour, not a regression.

confirmed.title: Rope-crossing completion has no guard/lock: a single crossing is re-scored and the round counter is advanced multiple times per crossing
confirmed.symptom: A player who cleanly walks to the far end of a rope does not simply advance one rope with a small score bump. Score inflates rapidly and the round counter jumps forward by several ropes at once from that single crossing — in one observed run (the realistic, non-forced-position test) the game even ended up on the 'gameover' screen immediately after what should have been a single clean successful crossing.
confirmed.rootCause: index.html:112-132, the `if(progress>=1){...}` block inside update(). On reaching the end of the rope it awards score and does `setTimeout(()=>{game.round++; if(game.round>=TOTAL){...state='won'}else{buildRound(game.round)}}, 400)` to advance to the next rope — but `game.state` stays 'playing' and `player.x`/`progress` are left exactly where they are until that deferred callback actually fires 400ms later. Nothing (no `transitioning`-style flag, no early return, no state change) stops update() from re-entering this same branch on every subsequent rendered frame during that 400ms window, each time awarding more score and scheduling another `game.round++` callback. Contrast with the fall-handling branch just above it (line ~101-108), which does have an explicit `return;` immediately after itself to prevent exactly this kind of re-entry.
confirmed.evidence: Deterministic repro: forced player.x to the finish line (880), then called update(16) four times back-to-back with zero real time elapsed. Score read 5, then 10, 15, 20 (expected: stay at 5, since no new input was given after the crossing completed). After sleeping 550ms to let the scheduled setTimeout callbacks fire, game.round read 4 (expected 1, for one rope crossed). Realistic corroboration with no forced position: a simple auto-balancing controller (lean opposite current tilt) walked the player to the finish naturally over 809 real update(16.7) calls; score at that exact moment was 3. Continuing to call update(16.7) for 24 more frames — matching the number of real 60fps browser frames that occur during any real 400ms wait, with no sleep inserted — pushed score to 15. After settling, game.round read 5 and game.state read 'gameover'. Because 400ms of real wall-clock time elapses between a real browser's requestAnimationFrame calls exactly as it does here, roughly 24 real frames occur in this window during ordinary play every time any rope is finished; this is not a synthetic timing artifact of the harness.
confirmed.fix:
Add a transition lock. Verified working in tests/_adv_tightrope_fixcheck.js (result: score=3, round=1, state=playing; 200 further frames confirm rope 2 plays normally at progress=0.307).

1. Declare near line 54: `let transitioning=false;`
2. In update(), immediately after `if(game.state!=='playing')return;` (line 73), add: `if(transitioning)return;`
3. In the crossing block, immediately before the setTimeout at line 132, add: `transitioning=true;`
4. In buildRound() (line 63), add: `transitioning=false;` alongside `game.state='playing';`

buildRound is the correct clear point because it is the single entry into any playable round, so it covers the round-advance callback, the fall/rebuild path, and restart via resetGame(). Clearing it only inside the setTimeout callback would NOT work: the `won` branch (game.round>=TOTAL) never calls buildRound, so the flag would stay stuck.

SECOND BROKEN PATH the obvious fix would miss: the death branch at line 105 has the same missing lock — `if(game.lives<=0){...sndLose();setTimeout(()=>game.state='gameover',400);}` also leaves state==='playing' and does NOT call buildRound, so for those 400ms update() keeps running with lives=0, the figure keeps walking, and it can even reach progress>=1 and score/advance a round while already dead. Set `transitioning=true;` there too (i.e. `sndLose();transitioning=true;setTimeout(...)`).

confirmed.title: retryRound() deletes a different round's already-recorded result
confirmed.symptom: After finishing round 1 and moving into round 2, if the player presses R (the documented 'retry round' key) partway through round 2, round 1's result silently disappears from the final accuracy summary shown on the win/gameover screen -- the game doesn't crash or freeze, but the stats it reports about a round the player actually completed are wrong.
confirmed.rootCause: retryRound() (index.html line 74) unconditionally calls game.roundResults.pop() to discard 'the round being retried', but a round's result is only pushed to roundResults by checkDone() when that round is FULLY completed. A round that is retried mid-way (the realistic case -- a player retries because they're doing badly, before finishing) has never pushed anything of its own, so the pop() removes the previous, already-completed round's entry instead.
confirmed.evidence: Test suite (test_timeline-sort.js, 'retryRound: mid-round retry must not corrupt history' block): after completing round 1 perfectly, game.game.roundResults.length === 1. With 2 of round 2's 5 cards placed (round 2 incomplete, nothing of its own in roundResults yet), calling g.retryRound() drops game.game.roundResults.length to 0 -- round 1's {round:1,correct:5,total:5} entry is gone. Source: `function retryRound(){if(game.state!=='playing')return;game.roundResults.pop();buildRound(game.round);}` has no check that the current round produced a result to discard.
confirmed.fix:
Guard the pop so it only fires when the last entry actually belongs to the round being rebuilt (line 74):

  function retryRound(){if(game.state!=='playing')return;const rr=game.roundResults;if(rr.length&&rr[rr.length-1].round===game.round)rr.pop();buildRound(game.round);}

Correct for both reachable cases (mid-round retry, and retry during the 900ms post-completion gap). Given the analysis it is equivalent to deleting the pop(); the guard is kept so the intent survives if checkDone's push/round++ ordering ever changes.

EXPLICIT CAVEAT — the obvious fix leaves a SECOND, different path broken: checkDone() schedules `setTimeout(()=>{if(game.state==='playing')buildRound(game.round);},900)`. Press R inside that window and retryRound() calls buildRound(game.round) immediately, then the pending timeout fires and calls buildRound(game.round) AGAIN, silently wiping cards placed in the first ~900ms of the retried round. That needs the timer cancelled (store the id, e.g. game.nextT, and clearTimeout(game.nextT) at the top of retryRound() and in resetGame()) — the roundResults guard does not cover it. Separately, retryRound() never refunds score/misses from the abandoned attempt; that is a design question, out of scope.

confirmed.title: Tap-to-continue leaves an uncancelled timer that double-advances the round
confirmed.symptom: A player answers a round and taps to skip the ~1s reveal (the game's own on-screen prompt: "tap to continue"). The tap correctly jumps to the next round immediately, but roughly a second later — with no further input — the round silently advances a second time on its own.
confirmed.rootCause: choose() (index.html, function choose(), body ends with `setTimeout(advanceRound,REVEAL_MS)`) schedules a real timer to auto-advance 900ms later. skipReveal() (index.html, `function skipReveal(){advanceRound();}`) is wired to onDown() as the tap-to-continue handler and calls advanceRound() immediately for the same answer, but never captures or clears the setTimeout id from choose(). advanceRound() itself (index.html, `function advanceRound(){game.round++; if(game.round>=TOTAL){...}else{buildRound(game.round);}}`) has no idempotency guard, so when the original timer later fires it blindly advances again.
confirmed.evidence: Test 'BUG CHECK: one answer + one skip should still equal exactly one round advance' failed. After answerCorrect() + skipReveal(), round went from 0 to 1 immediately (correct). After waiting past REVEAL_MS with zero further input, round became 2 on its own (console: "round silently advanced again on its own: 1 -> 2 with no further player input"). Reproduced identically on two separate full suite runs.

confirmed.title: Stray reveal timers from a tap-to-skip playthrough leak into the NEXT game after restart
confirmed.symptom: A player who plays through using tap-to-skip (the game's advertised way to move faster) reaches the win screen, immediately starts a new game, answers only the first question normally, and then — without touching anything else — watches their new game silently jump all the way to the win screen again.
confirmed.rootCause: Same missing-cancellation defect as above, at session scope: playing all 15 rounds via skipReveal() leaves up to 15 pending setTimeout(advanceRound) callbacks in flight (one per answered round, each still 900ms from its own choose() call). resetGame() (index.html, function resetGame()) does not track, cancel, or invalidate any outstanding timers — it only resets game state fields. Those old timers fire on whatever round happens to be live when they come due, which after a fast restart is the brand-new game.
confirmed.evidence: Test 'BUG CHECK: a game session with only one answer given should not auto-advance on its own' failed. After completing a 15-round game entirely via tap-to-skip and immediately calling resetGame() again, the new game started at round 0 as expected; after the player answered exactly one question in the new game and then 2000ms passed with no further input, round had jumped to 16 and game.state had become 'won' (console: "new game silently jumped from round 0 to round 16 and ended in state 'won' with no further input from the player"). Reproduced identically on two separate full suite runs, and confirmed distinct from the first finding by resetting the game between them and draining pending timers before this block began.

refuted.title: game.multiplier is initialized, clamped, and multiplied into every score calculation, but no code path anywhere ever increments it above 1
refuted.why:
The reported MECHANISM is factually correct and I reproduced it independently, but it is not a bug.

VERIFIED (my own jsdom probes, not the reporter's):
- Only 5 references to `multiplier` exist in the file: init 1 (:66), reset 1 in resetGame (:93), self-clamp `clamp(game.multiplier,1,4)` (:139), reset 1 on miss (:143), read at `(pts+bonus)*game.multiplier` (:145). No line assigns it >1.
- 8 forced dead-center throws (win.Math.random=()=>0.5): multiplier 1 throughout.
- 5000 randomized throws across ~500 full rounds exercising every ring and misses: max multiplier observed = 1.
- I additionally PATCHED OUT the separate ring-lookup bug so streaks could fire (streak reached 8, bonus scaling confirmed working): multiplier STILL 1. So the reporter's "inert even if the ring bug were fixed" claim also holds.

WHY IT IS NOT REAL:
1. No player-observable symptom. Multiplying by a permanent 1 is the identity operation. Every score the player sees equals the intended `pts + bonus`. The reporter concedes this ("None visible to a player"). The UI never mentions "multiplier".
2. The reward it appears to owe the player is already delivered by a working path: streaks ARE rewarded via `bonus = 5*(game.streak-1)` (:134), which I observed functioning (50->55->60->65...). Nothing is missing from the game.
3. Nothing is reachable because there is no state to reach — no wrong branch, no wrong number, no stuck state. A player cannot hit it. Fails the "would a real player hit this in normal play" test.
4. "Fixing" it would require INVENTING a mechanic (when should it rise, by how much, shown where?) — a feature request, not a fix. Shipping it would silently rebalance scoring and invalidate persisted localStorage best scores.

SEPARATE, OUT OF SCOPE BUT CONFIRMED: the ring-lookup at :125-127 iterates outermost-first with a break, so a dead-center bullseye scores "Awareness" (5pts) not "Clarity" (50). Inner rings unreachable, bestStreak stuck at 0, max score ~50 instead of 100. That one IS real and a player hits it every throw — but it is not the claim under review here.
refuted.artifact: dead code / vestigial variable — no player-observable symptom; a code-cleanup item, not a defect

refuted.title: Golden-bobber 2x bonus is not cleared by a noise/miss catch, only by a calm catch
refuted.why:
The MECHANISM is real and I reproduced it independently (my own script at C:\Users\admin\Claude\Projects\ANCF 100 HTML Games\tests\_adv_fish_golden_mine.js, driving only doCast/doReel/update — the exact calls handlePrimary() makes): game.golden survives a noise catch ("golden TRUE before, noise catch, golden still true"), survives the missed-dip branch ("MISS PATH: golden before=true after=true streak=0"), and survives the dip-timeout path in update() line 198 ("TIMEOUT PATH: golden before=true after=true streak=3"). So the reporter's code reading is accurate: only line 136, inside the calm-catch branch, ever sets golden=false.

But the behaviour is not a defect — it is coherent design. A full census of every `golden` reference (lines 49, 83, 124, 136, 137, 333-336) shows the flag is a BANKED ONE-SHOT TOKEN, not a streak-derived state: you earn it at streak%3===0, and line 136 is an explicit, deliberate CONSUME that spends it on your next calm catch. The `else if` on 137 correctly prevents earning a new token on the same catch that spends one. Nothing in the code, comments, or UI states or implies the token should expire with the streak — the reporter assumed that intent ("should be lost with the broken streak") and then reported the code for not matching an intent that exists only in the report.

No player-visible malfunction exists on any axis: the flag can never get stuck (any calm catch spends it — verified: 3 clean clears by calm catch in my run); the HUD never lies (line 336 prints "GOLDEN 2x" and the player genuinely receives mult=2 at line 124); it never blocks progression, never soft-locks, never re-earns wrongly, and never harms the player. The entire real-world impact is one catch scoring 40 instead of 23 (golden 2x vs comboMult 1.15 after a streak reset), at most ~3 times in a 10-fish round — and it favors the player. In a game explicitly themed on choosing calm over noise, a reward that waits for you rather than punishing a stumble is on-theme rather than broken.

The one genuinely odd thing nearby is game.goldenUsed (line 49/83): initialized and reset but never read or written anywhere else — a vestigial dead flag. That is cosmetic dead code, not the reported bug, and does not affect behaviour.

Verdict: reproducible mechanism, intentional design, no fix warranted. Changing it would alter game balance on the strength of an assumed intent.
refuted.artifact: intentional design (banked one-shot bonus token, not a streak-derived flag)

refuted.title: The recorded 'solution' hint() relies on is mathematically wrong on every locked-hex level (indices 6, 7, 8) -- replaying it literally leaves exactly one hex unsolved
refuted.why:
The MECHANISM is real and I reproduced it independently (my own jsdom script, not the reporter's): doFlipRaw() lines 114/119 make a locked cell toggle only on every 2nd hit, so its 'on' state is floor(hits/2)%2 -- period 4, not the period-2 XOR every unlocked cell has. Replaying the scramble doubles every cell's hit count to 2n; unlocked cells return to identity, but the locked cell lands on floor(2n/2)%2 = n%2, so it only reproduces when originally hit an EVEN number of times. My numbers match the reporter's exactly: lv6 lock=(2,2) hit 1x -> mismatch=1; lv7 lock=(4,2) hit 1x -> mismatch=1; lv8 lock=(7,1) hit 1x -> mismatch=1; lv9 lock=(2,4) hit 0x -> mismatch=0; unlocked lv0/1/5 -> mismatch=0. The line 101 comment "self-inverse: same sequence undoes the scramble" is genuinely false for locked levels.

But the IMPACT claim does not survive adversarial checking, on two independent grounds:

1. UNREACHABLE BY PLAY. I grepped every reference to `solution` in the shipped file -- there are exactly four: line 101 (the comment), line 102 (the write), line 151 (a length guard), line 152 (`const[c,r]=game.solution[game.solution.length-1]`). No code path anywhere replays the sequence. There is no auto-solve, no show-solution, no step-through. hint() reads exactly ONE element (the last) and never pops or advances it. So "replaying the recorded solution leaves one hex unsolved" is a state obtainable only by iterating game.solution yourself through the __cfq test hook -- precisely an internals poke the game never performs and a player has no route to. The repro in the report (`for (const [c,r] of g.game.solution) g.doFlip(c,r)`) IS the harness, not the game.

2. NO PLAYER-VISIBLE CONSEQUENCE. The levels are fully solvable -- I confirmed lv6/7/8 reach mismatch=0 and fire the real win transition (state='transition') after replay + 2 clicks on the locked hex; lv9 needs 0 extra. No soft-lock (the report concedes this itself). What a player pressing H actually sees is one highlighted hex; the game never hands them step 2, so whether the underlying 8-step plan is or isn't a true solution is entirely invisible to them. Crucially, the stale-hint behaviour is IDENTICAL on unlocked levels -- pressing H four times on lv6 returned (5,3) every time, and the same staleness occurs on lv0-5 where the solution data is provably correct. That proves the stale hint is NOT caused by the locked-hex math; it is the separate companion finding, and it is the only thing here that reaches a real player.

What remains is an incorrect code comment plus a latent defect in data the game does not consume the way the comment claims. That is not a bug a real player hits.

COUPLING WARNING (important for whoever fixes the companion finding): this defect is latent, not absent. The moment someone fixes the stale-hint bug by making hint() walk/pop game.solution step by step -- the obvious fix -- this becomes immediately real and player-facing, because the game would then hand players a sequence that provably does NOT solve levels 6, 7, and 8. Any fix to hint() that turns the array into a followable path MUST also fix the recorded solution (e.g. in buildLevel, after recording seq, append the locked hex twice if it was hit an odd number of times by the scramble; or make the locked cell's scramble contribution period-2 so the sequence is genuinely self-inverse). Fixing hint() alone would leave that second path broken.
refuted.artifact: unreachable-by-play / harness artifact: the "replay" is done by the test harness iterating game.solution via the __cfq hook. The shipped game never replays the sequence -- hint() reads only solution[last] and never advances -- so no player can reach the state. Underlying math defect is real but latent (wrong comment + unused-as-described data).

refuted.title: t
refuted.why:
THE CLAIM AS FILED IS NOT VERIFIABLE: every field is a single placeholder character. There is no described mechanism, no symptom, no evidence, and no repro, so it cannot be reproduced by construction. Per the rule "you cannot reproduce it", isReal=false for the report.

IMPORTANT — INDEPENDENT FINDING (not the report; surfaced so it is not lost): while reading the code path I found a genuine reachable crash and reproduced it myself in jsdom.

File: C:\Users\admin\ancf-digital-projects\games\script-scramble\index.html
Line 111 in startLevel(): const b=(ar+dr)*GRID_COLS+(ac+dc); [grid[a],grid[b]]=[grid[b],grid[a]];
The adjacency filter on line 108 bounds-checks against GRID_COLS/GRID_ROWS (full grid), but grid has length N=tiles.length. QUOTES[3] has 13 tiles on a 4x4 (16-cell) grid, so b can be 13..15 and grid[b] is undefined. My reproduction (jsdom, run from C:\Users\admin\Claude\Projects\ANCF 100 HTML Games\tests, looping startLevel(0..7)):

Player reachability: normal play. Finish levels 1-3, click for next -> handleClick -> startLevel(game.level+1) with lv=3 -> throws mid-function. tiles was already reassigned to the fresh unshuffled goal-order array and game.state='playing', so the rAF loop (draw is try/catch-wrapped) keeps rendering a fully green, pre-solved level 4 that never fires onWin, because checkWin() only runs from swapTiles. Not a harness artifact: no forced state, no poking internals, startLevel was called with its required argument, and the same call happens from the real click path.

refuted.title: The 'Garden Overrun' loss condition (choked >= MAX_CHOKED) appears unreachable through real play
refuted.why:
I reproduced a real GAMEOVER at choked=6/6 with my own harness, driving only clickPlot() + update() through the real loop.

WHY THE REPORTER MISSED IT. All three of their strategies starve the choke-eligible pool for incidental reasons, and they misread the mechanism. game.choked is a CUMULATIVE per-round counter that is never decremented — a choked plot stays counted even after it ripens away. The reporter's "leaves the choke-eligible pool" reasoning is about eligibility for NEW chokes, but the counter already banked is retained. The real limiter is choke EVENTS per round, and the reporter's bots suppressed them:
  - A (plant once, abandon): board is all-ripe by ~13s, only ~1 spawn tick occurs. Round never ends, so the counter never gets more events.
  - B (harvest instantly): each round ends after ~13-15s, i.e. ~2 spawn ticks max. Rounds are simply too SHORT to bank chokes — nothing to do with weeds.
  - C (hold at harvested=11): after 11 harvests the 15 plots are all permanently 'ripe' with no harvest budget left, so the board is inert. Plateau at 3 is an artifact of that specific cap, not a property of the game.

THE ACTUAL PATH. Line 159: targets = empty ∪ (growing && !choked). If a player plants every empty plot — the single most natural instinct in this game — the spawner has NO empty targets, so every spawn tick chokes a growing plant DIRECTLY (line 166) instead of creating a pullable weed. Rounds 4-5 (round>=3) spawn TWO per tick at a 6400ms interval. Combine that with a slightly longer round (batching harvests until a few plots are ripe — an ordinary human habit) and the chokes bank up.

MY REPRO (C:\Users\admin\Claude\Projects\ANCF 100 HTML Games\tests\_adv_seedgarden_probe.js). Bot plants every empty plot, PULLS EVERY WEED IT EVER SEES (fully diligent — not neglectful), harvests when N plots are ripe:
  batch=1 -> won, peak choked 4/6 (round 5)
  batch=2 -> won, peak choked 4/6 (round 5)
  batch=3,4,5,6,8,10 -> ALL reach state='gameover', choked=6/6, in round 5 at ~80s.
Confirmed genuine (_adv_seedgarden_confirm.js): state='gameover' persists across 800ms of real timers + 200 more update ticks (no transition timeout stomps it), clickPlot is inert, draw() renders the real 'Garden Overrun' panel, and resetGame() returns a playable board. Reached at harvested=11 in round 5 — one harvest short of winning, which is exactly the tension the loss condition is designed for.

So the loss is not just reachable, it is CLOSE for everyone: even the perfect instant-harvest player peaks at 4/6, and drawHUD line 260 fires its red warning flash at 5/6 — a screen a real player will see.

TWO NOTES, both out of scope of this claim and NOT bugs I'm asserting:
1. The gameover copy is misleading, not broken. In my losing run weedsPulled=0 and weedsSpread=0 — zero weeds ever existed, because a fully-planted board gives the spawner nothing but plants to choke. The screen still says "Too many plots were choked by neglected obligation weeds" / "Pull weeds promptly". That is a wrong-advice/wording issue on a working loss screen, not the reported unreachability.
2. The reporter's secondary transition-window race (update() still runs during 'transition' at line 124 and can set gameover, which the 500ms setTimeout then overwrites via buildRound) is a real but far narrower window, and they only produced it by direct data assignment. It does not bear on this claim, and my natural-play loss landed at harvested=11 with no transition pending.
refuted.artifact: could-not-reproduce / reporter's bot strategies were unrepresentative — the state IS reachable in normal play

refuted.title: Promised "won" screen is unreachable (dead state, not a soft-lock)
refuted.why:
I reproduced the reporter's facts independently (driving the real mousedown handler, not direct drop() calls): game.state is never assigned 'won' (grep shows only 'playing'/'gameover'/'paused'/'title'), 30 real clicked drops kept state at 'playing', and sndWin is defined but never called. However the CONCLUSION is wrong — this is intentional design, not a bug.

1) The title panel (index.html:337), which every player reads before the first tap (state starts 'title' and requires a click to begin), says verbatim: "Stack 15 blocks, then keep going in endless mode." The game tells the player upfront that 15 leads to endless mode. No win screen is promised where it counts.
2) The source comment (index.html:30) states the intent: "Stack 15 blocks to win, then endless mode continues."
3) The player IS clearly acknowledged at the target. My run confirmed game.endless flips at exactly block 15, spawning the 'ENDLESS MODE' popup (index.html:138-141), and the HUD switches to "Stack Blocks · Endless" with subtitle "Keep going — how high can you build?" (index.html:269-271). This is a designed milestone, not a silent no-op.

DECISIVE: the "obvious fix" would create a real bug. The dead won panel (index.html:340) says "Click / tap to keep building (endless)", but onDown (index.html:362) lumps 'won' in with 'title'/'gameover' and calls resetGame(). I forced state='won' after 5 drops and clicked: tower went 6 -> 1 and score 10 -> 0. Setting state='won' at TARGET would DESTROY the player's tower and zero their score at the moment of winning — the exact opposite of what the panel's own text promises.
